texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: ABSTRACT
texts.text: ABSTRACT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first light that escapes from a supernova explosion is the shock breakout emission, which produces a bright flash of UV or X-ray radiation. Standard theory predicts that the shock breakout spectrum will be a blackbody if the gas and radiation are in thermal equilibrium, or a Comptonized free-free spectrum if not. Using recent results for the post-breakout evolution which suggest that lower-temperature ejecta are probed earlier than previously thought, we show that another scenario is possible in which the gas and radiation are initially out of equilibrium, but the time when thermalized ejecta are revealed is short compared to the light-crossing time of the system. In this case, the observed spectrum differs significantly from the standard expectation, as the non-negligible light travel time acts to smear the spectrum into a complex multi-temperature blend of blackbody and free-free components. For typical parameters, a bright multi-wavelength transient is produced, with the free-free emission being spread over a wide frequency range from optical to hard X-rays, and the blackbody component peaking in soft X-rays. We explore the necessary conditions to obtain this type of unusual spectrum, finding that it may be relevant for bare blue supergiant progenitors, or for shocks with a velocity of 𝑣 bo ∼ 0 . 1 𝑐 breaking out from an extended medium of radius 𝑅 env with a sufficiently high density 𝜌 bo > ∼ 4 × 10 -12 g cm -3 ( 𝑅 env / 10 14 cm ) -15 / 16 . An application to low-luminosity gamma-ray bursts is considered in a companion paper.
texts.text: The first light that escapes from a supernova explosion is the shock breakout emission, which produces a bright flash of UV or X-ray radiation. Standard theory predicts that the shock breakout spectrum will be a blackbody if the gas and radiation are in thermal equilibrium, or a Comptonized free-free spectrum if not. Using recent results for the post-breakout evolution which suggest that lower-temperature ejecta are probed earlier than previously thought, we show that another scenario is possible in which the gas and radiation are initially out of equilibrium, but the time when thermalized ejecta are revealed is short compared to the light-crossing time of the system. In this case, the observed spectrum differs significantly from the standard expectation, as the non-negligible light travel time acts to smear the spectrum into a complex multi-temperature blend of blackbody and free-free components. For typical parameters, a bright multi-wavelength transient is produced, with the free-free emission being spread over a wide frequency range from optical to hard X-rays, and the blackbody component peaking in soft X-rays. We explore the necessary conditions to obtain this type of unusual spectrum, finding that it may be relevant for bare blue supergiant progenitors, or for shocks with a velocity of 𝑣 bo ∼ 0 . 1 𝑐 breaking out from an extended medium of radius 𝑅 env with a sufficiently high density 𝜌 bo > ∼ 4 × 10 -12 g cm -3 ( 𝑅 env / 10 14 cm ) -15 / 16 . An application to low-luminosity gamma-ray bursts is considered in a companion paper.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 INTRODUCTION
texts.text: 1 INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first emission observed from a supernova (SN) is a burst of emission escaping the radiation-mediated shock known as shock breakout. Characterizing the shock breakout emission has long been of theoretical interest, as observing the breakout pulse can provide crucial information about the progenitor star and its immediate environment. Building on the pioneering work of the 1970s (Colgate 1974; Weaver 1976; Falk 1978; Klein & Chevalier 1978), the subject has seen a resurgence since the late 2000s, as observational capabilities have evolved to probe ever shorter time-scales, finally bringing the detection of shock breakout within reach. Significant theoretical effort (see Waxman & Katz 2017, for a review) has been devoted to understanding the breakout signal in the classical case of a non-relativistic spherical explosion breaking out from a bare progenitor star (Imshennik & Nadezhin 1988; Ensman & Burrows 1992; Matzner & McKee 1999; Katz, Budnik, & Waxman 2010; Nakar & Sari 2010; Piro, Chang, & Weinberg 2010; Sapir, Katz, & Waxman 2011; Katz, Sapir, & Waxman 2012; Sapir, Katz, & Waxman 2013; Faran & Sari 2019), as well as extending these results to cover breakout in extended media (Waxman, Mészáros, & Campana 2007; Ofek et al. 2010; Balberg & Loeb 2011; Chevalier & Irwin 2011; Moriya et al. 2011; Chatzopoulos, Wheeler, & Vinko 2012; Ginzburg & Balberg 2012; Moriya & Tominaga 2012; Svirski, Nakar, & Sari 2012; Ginzburg & Balberg 2014; Svirski & Nakar 2014a,b; Moriya et al. 2018), relativistic shocks (Budnik et al. 2010; Nakar & Sari 2012; Granot, Nakar, & Levinson 2018; Faran & Sari 2023) and deviations from spherical symmetry (Couch et al. 2011; Matzner, Levin, & Ro 2013; Suzuki, Maeda, & Shigeyama 2016; Afsariardchi & Matzner 2018; Ohtani et al. 2018; Irwin et al. 2021; Goldberg, Jiang, & Bildsten 2022).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By now, several noteworthy shock breakout candidates have emerged; here we highlight only a few salient examples (for a more extensive discussion of candidates, see, e.g., Waxman & Katz 2017). Anearly milestone was the Swift discovery of prompt X-ray emission associated with the Type Ib SN 2008D (Mazzali et al. 2008; Soderberg et al. 2008; Malesani et al. 2009; Modjaz et al. 2009). Assuming this event was powered by shock breakout (see, however, Li 2008; Xu, Zou, & Fan 2008), the 600 s duration of the X-ray event significantly exceeded the expectation for a compact Type Ib progenitor, prompting the suggestion that the breakout took place in a stellar wind (e.g., Chevalier & Fransson 2008; Svirski & Nakar 2014b). At around the sametime, a new class of low-luminosity gamma-ray bursts ( 𝑙𝑙 GRBs) with faint and soft prompt emission was recognized (e.g., Liang et al. 2007; Virgili, Liang, & Zhang 2009, and references therein). Some of these events, like GRB 060218 (Campana et al. 2006; Mazzali et al. 2006; Pian et al. 2006; Soderberg et al. 2006; Sollerman et al. 2006; Amati et al. 2007; Kaneko et al. 2007) and GRB 100316D (Chornock et al. 2010; Cano et al. 2011; Fan et al. 2011; Starling et al. 2011; Margutti et al. 2013) have smooth, long-lived light curves distinct from the typical GRB population. Several authors have proposed explanations for these 𝑙𝑙 GRBs involving shock breakout (e.g., Campana et al. 2006; Li 2007; Wang et al. 2007; Waxman, Mészáros, &Campana 2007; Nakar & Sari 2012; Nakar 2015). Curiously, these events are also associated with broad-lined Type Ic SNe with a peculiar early optical peak (e.g., Campana et al. 2006), similar to the early peak seen in Type IIb events (see Modjaz, Gutiérrez, & Arcavi 2019, for a recent review of double-peaked SNe). This has been taken to indicate a non-standard progenitor with an extended, low-mass envelope (e.g., Nakar & Piro 2014; Nakar 2015; Irwin & Chevalier 2016). More recently, exquisite early data obtained from two nearby supernovae, SN 2023ixf (Hiramatsu et al. 2023; Hosseinzadeh et al. 2023; Jacobson-Galán et al. 2023; Smith et al. 2023; Teja et al. 2023; Vasylyev et al. 2023; Yamanaka, Fujii, & Nagayama 2023; Bostroem et al. 2024; Li et al. 2024; Shrestha et al. 2024; Singh et al. 2024; Zimmerman et al. 2024) and SN 2024ggi (Shrestha et al. 2024; Jacobson-Galán et al. 2024; Chen et al. 2024a,b; Pessi et al. 2024; Zhang et al. 2024), have shed new light on the early emission in Type II events. The long-lived bolometric peak, spectropolarimetric evolution, and presence of narrow emission features in these objects have painted the picture of a progenitor with a complex circumstellar environment, consistent with the pre-explosion imaging, which in both cases has revealed a dust-enshrouded red supergiant (Kilpatrick et al. 2023; Jencson et al. 2023; Xiang et al. 2024). These and other events have underscored the essential role played by the circumstellar environment in shaping the shock breakout emission.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, along with these revelations, there were also surprises. Of particular interest in the present work is the unusual spectrum of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs:someevents,mostnotablyGRB060218andGRB100316D, exhibit a peculiar spectrum with both a soft, ∼ 0 . 1 keV thermal component, and a hard, ∼ 10 keV power-law or Band function component (e.g., Campana et al. 2006; Kaneko et al. 2007; Toma et al. 2007; Starling et al. 2011). This complicated spectrum has led some authors to question whether a shock breakout origin is indeed possible for these events (e.g., Ghisellini, Ghirlanda, & Tavecchio 2007a,b; Irwin & Chevalier 2016).
texts.text: However, along with these revelations, there were also surprises. Of particular interest in the present work is the unusual spectrum of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs:someevents,mostnotablyGRB060218andGRB100316D, exhibit a peculiar spectrum with both a soft, ∼ 0 . 1 keV thermal component, and a hard, ∼ 10 keV power-law or Band function component (e.g., Campana et al. 2006; Kaneko et al. 2007; Toma et al. 2007; Starling et al. 2011). This complicated spectrum has led some authors to question whether a shock breakout origin is indeed possible for these events (e.g., Ghisellini, Ghirlanda, & Tavecchio 2007a,b; Irwin & Chevalier 2016).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aim of this paper is, first of all, to understand whether such a spectrum can ever be produced by shock breakout, and if so, to understand the requisite conditions and the overall spectral evolution. The structure of the paper is as follows. First, we introduce the basic idea of our model in Section 2. We start by reviewing the physics of non-equilibrium shock breakout in Section 2.1. After discussing the temperature evolution in Section 2.2, we show in Section 2.3 that a peculiar type of breakout can occur when the temperature evolution is sufficiently fast. Next, we investigate the necessary conditions for this non-standard shock breakout scenario in Section 3, considering the general theory in Section 3.1, and specifying to the case of shock breakout in extended dense environments in Section 3.2. A particularly interesting limiting case of our model and its connection to past work are discussed in Section 3.3 and Section 3.4. We then develop a model for the shock breakout spectrum in this new regime in Section 4. We describe the model parameters and assumptions in Section 4.1, the basic method for computing the spectrum in Section 4.2, and the effects of light travel time and Comptonization in Sections 4.3 and 4.4 respectively. We summarize our key results in Section 5, and conclude by discussing the outlook for observations of this unusual type of shock breakout. We also provide a glossary in Appendix A to help the reader keep track of the symbols defined throughout the paper.

We also provide a glossary in Appendix A to help the reader keep track of the symbols defined throughout the paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This is the first in a series of three papers related to shock breakout spectra. In the second paper (Irwin & Hotokezaka 2024a, submitted, hereafter Paper II), we consider a simple means of classifying the spectra produced by planar shock breakout and characterizing their time evolution. The third paper (Irwin & Hotokezaka 2024b, submitted, hereafter Paper III) considers an application of our results to a well-studied 𝑙𝑙 GRB, GRB 060218, which has long eluded a satisfactory explanation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 A NON-STANDARD SHOCK BREAKOUT SCENARIO
texts.text: 2 A NON-STANDARD SHOCK BREAKOUT SCENARIO

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Motivated by the observations of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs discussed in Section 1, our primary goal in this section is to answer a simple question: is it ever possible for a shock breakout to produce thermal and non-thermal emission simultaneously? To address this, we will first review the standard theoretical expectations for the initial breakout properties and the subsequent evolution. We then consider a thought experiment that leads to the answer we seek.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 Non-equilibrium breakout: basic idea and initial properties
texts.text: 2.1 Non-equilibrium breakout: basic idea and initial properties

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the general shock breakout problem, we consider a radiation mediated shock propagating in a dense region of size 𝑅 . The density profile near the edge of the region is assumed to steepen as 𝑟 → 𝑅 , with 𝜌 ( 𝑟 ) ∝ ( 𝑅 -𝑟 ) 𝑛 , causing the shock to accelerate leading up to the breakout. The shock's acceleration is described by the self-similar solution of Sakurai (1960), which shows that the velocity scales with density as 𝑣 ∝ 𝜌 -𝜇 , with 𝜇 ≈ 0 . 19. Shock breakout occurs when the diffusion time from the shock to the surface becomes equal to the time it takes for the shock to cross that same distance; this happens at an optical depth of 𝑐 / 𝑣 bo , where 𝑣 bo is the shock velocity at the moment of breakout and 𝑐 is the speed of light. We treat here the case where the breakout occurs close to the edge of the medium, so that the shock radius at breakout, 𝑅 bo , satisfies 𝑅 bo ≈ 𝑅 and 𝑅 bo -𝑅 ≪ 𝑅 . This is typical of breakout from the edge of a star, but also applies to breakout from an extended medium, if the medium has a sharp edge and is sufficiently optically thick (e.g., Chevalier & Irwin 2011; Haynie & Piro 2021; Khatami & Kasen 2024, see also Section 3 below).

Shock breakout occurs when the diffusion time from the shock to the surface becomes equal to the time it takes for the shock to cross that same distance; this happens at an optical depth of 𝑐 / 𝑣 bo , where 𝑣 bo is the shock velocity at the moment of breakout and 𝑐 is the speed of light. We treat here the case where the breakout occurs close to the edge of the medium, so that the shock radius at breakout, 𝑅 bo , satisfies 𝑅 bo ≈ 𝑅 and 𝑅 bo -𝑅 ≪ 𝑅 . texts.label: text
texts.orig: To aid the discussion, it will be helpful to introduce some terminology. Following Nakar & Sari (2010, hereafter NS10), we define three special locations within the breakout ejecta: the 'breakout shell,' the 'luminosity shell,' and the 'colour shell.' As in Faran & Sari (2019, hereafter FS19), we will label the properties of these shells using the subscripts 'bo,' 'ls,' and 'cs' respectively. The breakout shell is the shell from which the first breakout radiation escapes, and its properties determine the initial conditions in the problem. The luminosity shell is the shell from which the observed photons originate at a given time, i.e. it is the shell which satisfies the condition 𝜏 ≈ 𝑐 / 𝑣 (up to a logarithmic correction, as discussed below). Initially, the luminosity shell coincides with the breakout shell, but as time goes on and progressively more of the ejecta are exposed, it recedes inwards. Thus, after the initial breakout flash, the luminosity shell is always interior to the breakout shell. Finally, the colour shell is the shell out to which the gas can modify the spectrum of the radiation, i.e. it is the shell where the observed temperature is determined. Its location depends on whether the luminosity shell is in thermal equilibrium or not (NS10). If it is, then the colour shell is located outside the luminosity shell, at the outermost location where thermal equilibrium can be maintained (i.e., the thermalization depth). Otherwise, the colour shell is simply the luminosity shell.

Following Nakar & Sari (2010, hereafter NS10), we define three special locations within the breakout ejecta: the 'breakout shell,' the 'luminosity shell,' and the 'colour shell.' As in Faran & Sari (2019, hereafter FS19), we will label the properties of these shells using the subscripts 'bo,' 'ls,' and 'cs' respectively. The breakout shell is the shell from which the first breakout radiation escapes, and its properties determine the initial conditions in the problem. The luminosity shell is the shell from which the observed photons originate at a given time, i.e. it is the shell which satisfies the condition 𝜏 ≈ 𝑐 / 𝑣 (up to a logarithmic correction, as discussed below). Initially, the luminosity shell coincides with the breakout shell, but as time goes on and progressively more of the ejecta are exposed, it recedes inwards. Thus, after the initial breakout flash, the luminosity shell is always interior to the breakout shell. Finally, the colour shell is the shell out to which the gas can modify the spectrum of the radiation, i.e. it is the shell where the observed temperature is determined. Its location depends on whether the luminosity shell is in thermal equilibrium or not (NS10). If it is, then the colour shell is located outside the luminosity shell, at the outermost location where thermal equilibrium can be maintained (i.e., the thermalization depth). Otherwise, the colour shell is simply the luminosity shell.

texts.orig: An unexplored regime of shock breakout
texts.text: An unexplored regime of shock breakout

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The thermal equilibrium conditions at breakout can be gauged by utilizing the thermal coupling coefficient 𝜂 introduced by NS10. Effectively, 𝜂 is the ratio of the photon number density required to achieve thermal equilibrium in a given shell, to the number density of photons with energy ≈ 3 𝑘𝑇 BB generated by free-free processes in that shell. 1 If 𝜂 < 1, then enough photons are generated to reach thermal equilibrium, and the radiation temperature is equal to the blackbody temperature of the shell, 𝑇 BB. On the other hand, if 𝜂 > 1, then thermal equilibrium cannot be attained and the radiation temperature is > 𝑇 BB.
texts.text: The thermal equilibrium conditions at breakout can be gauged by utilizing the thermal coupling coefficient 𝜂 introduced by NS10. Effectively, 𝜂 is the ratio of the photon number density required to achieve thermal equilibrium in a given shell, to the number density of photons with energy ≈ 3 𝑘𝑇 BB generated by free-free processes in that shell. 1 If 𝜂 < 1, then enough photons are generated to reach thermal equilibrium, and the radiation temperature is equal to the blackbody temperature of the shell, 𝑇 BB. On the other hand, if 𝜂 > 1, then thermal equilibrium cannot be attained and the radiation temperature is > 𝑇 BB.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The thermal coupling coefficient of the breakout shell, 𝜂 bo , is an important quantity because it determines whether or not the observed radiation is initially in thermal equilibrium with the gas, which strongly influences the observed spectrum. If 𝜂 bo < 1, the gas and radiation are in thermal equilibrium from the beginning, and the observed spectrum is a blackbody. However, if 𝜂 bo > 1, the gas and radiation are initially out of equilibrium, and the observed spectrum is a self-absorbed free-free spectrum, which may also be modified by Comptonization (NS10). In this case, blackbody emission is not observed until later, when slower and denser layers of the ejecta which can maintain equilibrium have been revealed to the observer.
texts.text: The thermal coupling coefficient of the breakout shell, 𝜂 bo , is an important quantity because it determines whether or not the observed radiation is initially in thermal equilibrium with the gas, which strongly influences the observed spectrum. If 𝜂 bo < 1, the gas and radiation are in thermal equilibrium from the beginning, and the observed spectrum is a blackbody. However, if 𝜂 bo > 1, the gas and radiation are initially out of equilibrium, and the observed spectrum is a self-absorbed free-free spectrum, which may also be modified by Comptonization (NS10). In this case, blackbody emission is not observed until later, when slower and denser layers of the ejecta which can maintain equilibrium have been revealed to the observer.

texts.orig: The parameter 𝑞 in equation 2 is included to account for possible uncertainties in the value of 𝜂 bo . As we will see, some quantities in our model are quite sensitive to the value of 𝜂 bo , so it is important to keep track of how order-unity variations in 𝜂 bo affect our results. Equation 2 agrees with equation 10 in NS10 when 𝑞 = 1 and the factor of ( 𝑛 + 1 ) -1 𝑔 -1 B is dropped. However, as pointed out by NS10, the value obtained by Katz, Budnik, & Waxman (2010) is effectively twice as large, corresponding to 𝑞 ≈ 2. This difference possibly stems from the fact that Katz, Budnik, & Waxman (2010) obtained the same scaling for the blackbody temperature, but with a different numerical prefactor: 𝑇 BB , bo ≈ ( 2 . 6 𝜌 bo 𝑣 2 bo / 𝑎 ) 1 / 4 according to their equation 5. Although this difference only slightly affects 𝑇 BB , bo , since 𝜂 bo ∝ 𝑇 7 / 2 BB , bo via equation 9 of NS10, the value of 𝜂 bo is about doubled. On the other hand, if bound-free or other photon generation processes contribute non-negligible numbers of photons, the value of 𝜂 bo will be reduced compared to the value of NS10 which only includes free-free photon production. A steep density profile also leads to a reduction in 𝜂 bo via the ( 1 + 𝑛 ) -1 prefactor (for the same 𝑣 bo and 𝜌 bo ). Later, we will argue that a value of 𝑞 ( 𝑛 + 1 ) -1 ∼ 0 . 5 leads to good agreement with Monte-Carlo simulations of radiative shocks (e.g., Ito et al. 2018; Ito, Levinson, & Nagataki 2020; Ito, Levinson, & Nakar 2020, see Section 4.4 for further discussion).

However, as pointed out by NS10, the value obtained by Katz, Budnik, & Waxman (2010) is effectively twice as large, corresponding to 𝑞 ≈ 2. This difference possibly stems from the fact that Katz, Budnik, & Waxman (2010) obtained the same scaling for the blackbody temperature, but with a different numerical prefactor: 𝑇 BB , bo ≈ ( 2 . 6 𝜌 bo 𝑣 2 bo / 𝑎 ) 1 / 4 according to their equation 5. Although this difference only slightly affects 𝑇 BB , bo , since 𝜂 bo ∝ 𝑇 7 / 2 BB , bo via equation 9 of NS10, the value of 𝜂 bo is about doubled. On the other hand, if bound-free or other photon generation processes contribute non-negligible numbers of photons, the value of 𝜂 bo will be reduced compared to the value of NS10 which only includes free-free photon production. A steep density profile also leads to a reduction in 𝜂 bo via the ( 1 + 𝑛 ) -1 prefactor (for the same 𝑣 bo and 𝜌 bo ). texts.orig: by equations 2 and 3, and 𝑘 is Boltzmann's constant. In some parts of parameter space, it is possible to eliminate 𝜉 bo from equation 8 to obtain an implicit equation for 𝑇 obs , bo in terms of 𝑣 bo and 𝑞 /( 𝑛 + 1 ) only (see Section 4.4). Note that equation 8 is only applicable when the observed temperature is below the energy threshold for pair creation. Wewill assume that this is the case throughout this paper. Once pairs become important ( 𝑇 obs , bo > ∼ 50 keV), the temperature rises much more slowly with 𝑣 bo and it eventually saturates around ≈ 200 keV (see Weaver 1976; Katz, Budnik, & Waxman 2010; Nakar & Sari 2012; Faran & Sari 2023, for further discussion). Although the pair creation condition is marginal for GRB 060218, which had a peak energy of ∼ 30 keV (Campana et al. 2006; Toma et al. 2007; Kaneko et al. 2007), a recent analysis by Faran & Sari (2023) concluded that relativistic effects are most likely unimportant for this event.

Although the pair creation condition is marginal for GRB 060218, which had a peak energy of ∼ 30 keV (Campana et al. 2006; Toma et al. 2007; Kaneko et al. 2007), a recent analysis by Faran & Sari (2023) concluded that relativistic effects are most likely unimportant for this event.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If we assume that 𝑙𝑙 GRBs are powered by shock breakout, then for an observed peak energy of 3 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo ∼ 30 keV, as is typical in these events, equation 8 implies 𝑣 bo > ∼ 0 . 1 𝑐 . Due to the extreme sensitivity of 𝑇 obs , bo on 𝑣 bo , the breakout velocity cannot be much larger than this value unless the Comptonization parameter 𝜉 bo is very large. Even if 𝜉 bo = 100, the implied breakout velocity is only 𝑣 bo ≈ 0 . 3 𝑐 for the same observed temperature. Moreover, observations seem to disfavour a large Comptonization, as this would lead to a prominent Wien peak in the spectrum, which so far has not been observed in any 𝑙𝑙 GRB. We therefore conclude that if 𝑙𝑙 GRBs are powered by shock breakout, the shock velocity must have been ≈ 0 . 1 𝑐 , consistent with the discussion in Paper III. We will return to the topic of Comptonization when discussing our spectral model in Section 4.

texts.orig: Since the initial observed temperature also depends very strongly on the breakout velocity, 𝑇 obs , bo ∝ 𝑣 8 bo according to equation 8, systems with fast shocks that produce out-of-equilibrium breakouts will inevitably have a very large separation between ℎ𝜈 a , bo and 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo , and therefore will emit over a very wide range in frequencies. As seen from equation 10, in low-density environments ℎ𝜈 a , bo can easily be below the optical band, while 𝑘𝑇 bo can be tens of keV via equation 8, resulting in a transient that is bright from optical up to hard X-rays. A similar conclusion about enhanced optical/UV emission was reached by Ito, Levinson, & Nakar (2020), who considered radiation-mediated shocks in stellar winds.

texts.text: 2.2 Non-equilibrium breakout: temperature evolution and rapid thermalization

texts.orig: Early works on shock breakout (e.g., NS10) assumed that the luminosity shell is always found at the location where the optical depth is 𝜏 ≈ 𝑐 / 𝑣 . Because the breakout shell satisfies this condition at 𝑡 = 𝑡 bo , and because the optical depth is approximately constant during the early planar phase of evolution, this assumption implies that the luminosity shell is equivalent to the breakout shell during the entire planar phase, i.e. 𝑚 ls ≈ 𝑚 bo for 𝑡 < 𝑡 s , where 𝑚 ls and 𝑚 bo are respectively the mass coordinates of the luminosity and breakout shells, and

Because the breakout shell satisfies this condition at 𝑡 = 𝑡 bo , and because the optical depth is approximately constant during the early planar phase of evolution, this assumption implies that the luminosity shell is equivalent to the breakout shell during the entire planar phase, i.e. 𝑚 ls ≈ 𝑚 bo for 𝑡 < 𝑡 s , where 𝑚 ls and 𝑚 bo are respectively the mass coordinates of the luminosity and breakout shells, and

texts.orig: is the time it takes for the breakout shell's radius to double. However, recent work by FS19 has shown that this assumption is not quite correct. By obtaining a self-similar solution describing the specific energy in the outer layers of the ejecta, they demonstrated that 𝑚 ls actually grows logarithmically with time during the planar phase as 𝑚 ls = 𝑚 bo [ 1 + ln ( 𝑡 / 𝑡 bo )] ( 𝑛 + 1 )/( 𝑛 + 1 -𝜇𝑛 ) . With this correction, the luminosity shell is located slightly deeper in the ejecta, at an optical depth of 𝜏 ls ≈ ( 𝑐 / 𝑣 ls ) [ 1 + ln ( 𝑡 / 𝑡 bo )] rather than at 𝜏 ls ≈ 𝑐 / 𝑣 ls , where 𝑣 ls is the luminosity shell's velocity.
texts.text: is the time it takes for the breakout shell's radius to double. However, recent work by FS19 has shown that this assumption is not quite correct. By obtaining a self-similar solution describing the specific energy in the outer layers of the ejecta, they demonstrated that 𝑚 ls actually grows logarithmically with time during the planar phase as 𝑚 ls = 𝑚 bo [ 1 + ln ( 𝑡 / 𝑡 bo )] ( 𝑛 + 1 )/( 𝑛 + 1 -𝜇𝑛 ) . With this correction, the luminosity shell is located slightly deeper in the ejecta, at an optical depth of 𝜏 ls ≈ ( 𝑐 / 𝑣 ls ) [ 1 + ln ( 𝑡 / 𝑡 bo )] rather than at 𝜏 ls ≈ 𝑐 / 𝑣 ls , where 𝑣 ls is the luminosity shell's velocity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This logarithmic correction has important implications for the observed behaviour at early times. Although the effect on the luminosity is minor (to a good approximation, 𝐿 ls ∝ 𝑡 -4 / 3 throughout the planar phase with an extremely weak dependence on logarithmic terms, see FS19), the effect on the temperature can be drastic, particularly if the breakout is initially out of thermal equilibrium, with 𝜂 bo > 1. As the luminosity shell moves into the ejecta, it encounters denser and slower ejecta, which are more readily thermalized. Because the thermal coupling coefficient depends strongly on location in the outer parts of the flow, even a small change in the luminosity shell's position can significantly influence the spectrum (recall that when 𝜂 > 1, the luminosity shell is equivalent to the colour shell, i.e. the properties of the luminosity shell fully determine the observed spectrum). Therefore, compared to previous models which neglect the logarithmic dependence of 𝑚 ls , thermal equilibrium of the luminosity shell can be achieved much sooner in the model of FS19. This can be seen by examining the evolution of the luminosity shell's thermal coupling coefficient, which in the planar phase is given by (FS19)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thermal equilibrium of the luminosity shell is achieved once 𝜂 ls becomes equal to unity. Let us define the time when this occurs as 𝑡 eq , ls . Since the luminosity shell determines the observed spectrum during the non-equilibrium phase (as discussed above), the time 𝑡 eq , ls is also the time when the observer first starts to see blackbody emission. For a typical shock breakout occurring at the edge of a standard blue supergiant or Wolf-Rayet star progenitor, the breakout is initially out of equilibrium (i.e., 𝜂 bo > 1), and it remains that way throughout the planar phase (NS10,FS19). In other words, 𝑡 eq , ls > 𝑡 s in the usual case. However, we point out that in principle, 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 s may also be possible if the progenitor's properties differ somewhat from the typically assumed values. In fact, as we will see below, in some parts of parameter space 𝑡 eq , ls can even be shorter than the light-crossing time of the system, 𝑡 lc (see Section 3.1).

texts.orig: Figure 1. An illustration of the system's geometry when 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 lc . A source of size 𝑅 produces a flash of free-free emission lasting for a time 𝑡 eq , ls (shown in blue), and subsequently radiates as a blackbody (shown in red). Each panel shows where the radiation has reached at a different time. The black dashed line indicates the slice probed by the observer at time 𝑡 . The inset shows the emission regions that an observer who could resolve the source would see. Panel a): For times 𝑡 < 𝑡 eq , ls , the source produces free-free emission. The leading edge of the radiation has travelled a distance 𝑐𝑡 , as indicated by the dark green bar. Only a small part of the surface, which is within a distance of 𝑐𝑡 from the dashed line (corresponding to angles within 𝜃 < ∼ ( 2 𝑡 / 𝑡 lc ) 1 / 2 of the line of sight), can be observed. Panel b): For times 𝑡 > 𝑡 eq , ls , the surface is radiating as a blackbody. The blackbody emission has travelled a distance of 𝑐 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls ) , as shown by the purple bar. Only the blackbody radiation emitted within a distance of 𝑐 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls ) from the dashed line (or within an angle of 𝜃 < ∼ [ 2 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls )/ 𝑡 lc ] 1 / 2 ) has had time to reach the observer. For 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , free-free emission is observed from a ring, with [ 2 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls )/ 𝑡 lc ] 1 / 2 < ∼ 𝜃 < ∼ ( 2 𝑡 / 𝑡 lc ) 1 / 2 , while emission from near the equator, which has travelled a distance of 𝑐𝑡 < 𝑅 , still has not arrived. The free-free emission produced at the equator finally arrives at 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc and it lasts for a time 𝑡 eq , ls after that. Panel c): At 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , the blackbody emission from the equator finally arrives. From this point on, the entire source is visible and only blackbody radiation is observed.

Panel c): At 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , the blackbody emission from the equator finally arrives. From this point on, the entire source is visible and only blackbody radiation is observed.

texts.orig: With the results of Sections 2.1 and 2.2, we are now equipped to address the question posed at the beginning of this section. Since we have seen that shock breakout either produces blackbody or freefree emission, we can rephrase the question as: do we ever expect to see both of these at the same time? Let us consider what would be required for this to happen.
texts.text: With the results of Sections 2.1 and 2.2, we are now equipped to address the question posed at the beginning of this section. Since we have seen that shock breakout either produces blackbody or freefree emission, we can rephrase the question as: do we ever expect to see both of these at the same time? Let us consider what would be required for this to happen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First of all, as we saw in Section 2.2, in order to observe free-free emission, we require that 𝑡 eq , ls > 𝑡 bo so that the gas and radiation are initially out of equilibrium. We also saw that in this case, blackbody emission is only produced at times 𝑡 > 𝑡 eq , ls . Meanwhile, as discussed in Section 2.1, the observed duration of the breakout flash is 𝑡 obs = max ( 𝑡 bo , 𝑡 lc ) . Therefore, in order to see blackbody emission while the breakout is ongoing, we require 𝑡 eq , ls < max ( 𝑡 bo , 𝑡 lc ) . Clearly, this condition contradicts the one above if the right hand side equals 𝑡 bo , so to avoid this we must take 𝑡 lc > 𝑡 bo . The condition for seeing blackbody emission during the breakout then becomes 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 lc . Finally, combining the conditions for seeing blackbody and free-free emission, we have

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Assuming that equation 26 holds, how would the observed emission be affected? Let us first clarify how the spectrum is impacted by light travel time. As shown in equations 13-18, the quantities shaping the spectrum evolve on a characteristic time-scale of 𝑡 bo . If the light-crossing time were negligible (i.e., if 𝑡 lc ≪ 𝑡 bo ) , then the observed spectrum would be a blackbody or free-free spectrum described by a single characteristic temperature, 𝑇 obs , ls ( 𝑡 ) . The effect of a non-negligible light-crossing time (i.e., 𝑡 lc > ∼ 𝑡 bo ) is to smear this spectrum over a range of temperatures (for further details, see Section 4.3). This occurs because, at a given observer time, the emission arriving from close to the line of sight originates from deeper layers of the ejecta compared to the emission from higher latitudes, which arrives with a delay. Since the deeper layers are also cooler, we expect to simultaneously see cooler emission coming from near the line of sight, and hotter emission coming from higher latitudes.
texts.text: Assuming that equation 26 holds, how would the observed emission be affected? Let us first clarify how the spectrum is impacted by light travel time. If the light-crossing time were negligible (i.e., if 𝑡 lc ≪ 𝑡 bo ) , then the observed spectrum would be a blackbody or free-free spectrum described by a single characteristic temperature, 𝑇 obs , ls ( 𝑡 ) . The effect of a non-negligible light-crossing time (i.e., 𝑡 lc > ∼ 𝑡 bo ) is to smear this spectrum over a range of temperatures (for further details, see Section 4.3). This occurs because, at a given observer time, the emission arriving from close to the line of sight originates from deeper layers of the ejecta compared to the emission from higher latitudes, which arrives with a delay. Since the deeper layers are also cooler, we expect to simultaneously see cooler emission coming from near the line of sight, and hotter emission coming from higher latitudes.

texts.orig: so at a given time 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , only emission from within 𝜃 < ∼ √︁ 2 𝑡 / 𝑡 lc has had enough time to reach the observer. Initially, at times 𝑡 < 𝑡 eq , ls , we would see emission only from the outermost layers of the ejecta, where the gas and radiation are not in equilibrium (as in the left panel of Fig. 1). The expected spectrum would be a Comptonized free-free spectrum, although smeared by light-crossing effects as discussed above (see also Section 4). However, at time 𝑡 = 𝑡 eq , ls , ejecta which are in thermal equilibrium would start to be revealed along the line of sight, and a blackbody component would emerge in the spectrum. Then, for times 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , the emitting material within 𝜃 < ∼ √︃ 2 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls )/ 𝑡 lc of the line of sight would have already reached thermal equilibrium, while the emitting material beyond that would still be out of equilibrium, resulting in an observed spectrum with both blackbody and free-free components, as in the central panel of Fig. 1. Finally, at times 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , all of the observed material would be in equilibrium, as in the right panel of Fig. 1. The freefree component would fade, and the observed spectrum would be a blackbody.
texts.text: so at a given time 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , only emission from within 𝜃 < ∼ √︁ 2 𝑡 / 𝑡 lc has had enough time to reach the observer. Initially, at times 𝑡 < 𝑡 eq , ls , we would see emission only from the outermost layers of the ejecta, where the gas and radiation are not in equilibrium (as in the left panel of Fig. 1). The expected spectrum would be a Comptonized free-free spectrum, although smeared by light-crossing effects as discussed above (see also Section 4). However, at time 𝑡 = 𝑡 eq , ls , ejecta which are in thermal equilibrium would start to be revealed along the line of sight, and a blackbody component would emerge in the spectrum. Then, for times 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , the emitting material within 𝜃 < ∼ √︃ 2 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls )/ 𝑡 lc of the line of sight would have already reached thermal equilibrium, while the emitting material beyond that would still be out of equilibrium, resulting in an observed spectrum with both blackbody and free-free components, as in the central panel of Fig. 1. Finally, at times 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , all of the observed material would be in equilibrium, as in the right panel of Fig. 1. The freefree component would fade, and the observed spectrum would be a blackbody.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, the answer to our question is yes, it is possible to see both blackbody and free-free emission at the same time in shock breakout, for times 𝑡 eq , ls < ∼ 𝑡 < ∼ 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , provided that equation 26 can be satisfied. For convenience, we will refer to this type of non-standard shock breakout as 'initially non-equilibrium rapid thermalization breakout,' or INERT breakout for short. The INERT case is distinguished from the usual non-equilibrium case by a thermalization time-scale which is short compared to the light-crossing time. To highlight this distinction, we will refer to the standard non-equilibrium breakout scenario, which produces only free-free emission during the breakout flash, as 'non-equilibrium slow thermalization breakout,' or NEST breakout. As for the classical case where equilibrium holds from the beginning, we simply call this 'thermal breakout.'
texts.text: Thus, the answer to our question is yes, it is possible to see both blackbody and free-free emission at the same time in shock breakout, for times 𝑡 eq , ls < ∼ 𝑡 < ∼ 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , provided that equation 26 can be satisfied. For convenience, we will refer to this type of non-standard shock breakout as 'initially non-equilibrium rapid thermalization breakout,' or INERT breakout for short. The INERT case is distinguished from the usual non-equilibrium case by a thermalization time-scale which is short compared to the light-crossing time. To highlight this distinction, we will refer to the standard non-equilibrium breakout scenario, which produces only free-free emission during the breakout flash, as 'non-equilibrium slow thermalization breakout,' or NEST breakout. As for the classical case where equilibrium holds from the beginning, we simply call this 'thermal breakout.'

texts.orig: The parameter 𝜒 encodes whether the breakout duration is set by the light-crossing time ( 𝜒 > 1) or by the diffusion time of the breakout shell ( 𝜒 < 1), while the parameter 𝜁 expresses the thermal equilibrium time-scale as a fraction of the duration of the breakout. With these definitions, in order to obtain an INERT breakout spectrum we require 𝜁 < 1 and 𝜒 > 1 while 𝜂 bo > 1.
texts.text: The parameter 𝜒 encodes whether the breakout duration is set by the light-crossing time ( 𝜒 > 1) or by the diffusion time of the breakout shell ( 𝜒 < 1), while the parameter 𝜁 expresses the thermal equilibrium time-scale as a fraction of the duration of the breakout. With these definitions, in order to obtain an INERT breakout spectrum we require 𝜁 < 1 and 𝜒 > 1 while 𝜂 bo > 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our aim in this section is to determine how the dimensionless parameters 𝜂 bo , 𝜒 , and 𝜁 vary throughout the shock breakout parameter space. Throughout this study, we fix the values of 𝜇 = 0 . 19 and 𝜅 = 0 . 2 cm 2 g -1 . 5 For simplicity, we also take 𝑔 ff ≈ 𝑔 B ≈ 1 for the remainder of the paper. In this case, the behaviour is determined by four independent parameters. We will first consider the general case in Section 3.1, taking 𝑣 bo , 𝜌 bo , 𝑅 bo , and 𝑛 as the four parameters. The motivation for choosing 𝜌 bo and 𝑣 bo is that in principle, if sufficiently early spectra were available, they could be constrained directly from observations. Additionally, this parametrization is convenient because 𝜂 bo effectively depends on only one parameter, 𝑣 bo , with only a very weak dependence on 𝜌 bo and no dependence on 𝑅 bo or 𝑛 . After studying the general case, we will specify to the case of breakout from an extended envelope in Section 3.2, and consider how the breakout depends on the envelope's mass and radius, and the deposited energy.

texts.orig: These relationships between 𝜒 , 𝜁 , and 𝜂 bo also have a geometric interpretation. Let the value of 𝑛 be fixed, and consider the 3dimensional parameter space ( 𝜌 bo , 𝑣 bo , 𝑅 bo ) . In this space, the equations 𝜁 = 1, 𝜒 = 1, and 𝜂 bo = 1 each define 2-dimensional surfaces. We have shown that if two of these equations are satisfied, then the third is also satisfied automatically; this means that all three surfaces meet in a single 1-dimensional curve. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The 𝜁 = 1 surface is curved and more complicated, but it still passes through the same line. Now, since 𝜁 < 1 is not possible if 𝜒 ≤ 1 (see Fig. 2), the 𝜁 = 1 surface is only defined on one side of the 𝜒 = 1 plane, where 𝜒 > 1. Furthermore, for 𝜒 > 1, we have shown that when 𝜁 = 1, 𝜂 bo = 𝜂 bo ,𝜁 = 1 > 1 via equation 32. This means that the 𝜁 = 1 surface also lies entirely on the 𝜂 bo > 1 side of the 𝜂 bo = 1 plane. So, the 𝜒 = 1 and 𝜂 bo = 1 planes divide the space into four regions, and the 𝜁 = 1 surface further subdivides the region where 𝜒 > 1 and 𝜂 bo > 1 into two subregions where 𝜁 < 1 or 𝜁 > 1, creating five regions in total. The wedge-shaped region between the 𝜂 bo = 1 plane and the 𝜁 = 1 surface, on the 𝜒 > 1 side of the 𝜒 = 1 plane, is where an INERT breakout is achieved.

Now, since 𝜁 < 1 is not possible if 𝜒 ≤ 1 (see Fig. 2), the 𝜁 = 1 surface is only defined on one side of the 𝜒 = 1 plane, where 𝜒 > 1. Furthermore, for 𝜒 > 1, we have shown that when 𝜁 = 1, 𝜂 bo = 𝜂 bo ,𝜁 = 1 > 1 via equation 32. This means that the 𝜁 = 1 surface also lies entirely on the 𝜂 bo > 1 side of the 𝜂 bo = 1 plane. So, the 𝜒 = 1 and 𝜂 bo = 1 planes divide the space into four regions, and the 𝜁 = 1 surface further subdivides the region where 𝜒 > 1 and 𝜂 bo > 1 into two subregions where 𝜁 < 1 or 𝜁 > 1, creating five regions in total. The wedge-shaped region between the 𝜂 bo = 1 plane and the 𝜁 = 1 surface, on the 𝜒 > 1 side of the 𝜒 = 1 plane, is where an INERT breakout is achieved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To summarize, the 3-dimensional parameter space can be broken into the following 5 distinct regions with different spectral properties (for more about the spectra in each case, see Section 4, as well as Paper II):
texts.text: To summarize, the 3-dimensional parameter space can be broken into the following 5 distinct regions with different spectral properties (for more about the spectra in each case, see Section 4, as well as Paper II):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (i) 𝜒 > 1, 𝜂 bo < 1, and 𝜁 < 1: The breakout is in equilibrium, with the duration set by the light-crossing time, and the spectrum is a blackbody.
texts.text: (i) 𝜒 > 1, 𝜂 bo < 1, and 𝜁 < 1: The breakout is in equilibrium, with the duration set by the light-crossing time, and the spectrum is a blackbody.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (ii) 𝜒 > 1, 𝜂 bo > 1, and 𝜁 < 1: The breakout is out of equilibrium, with the duration set by the light-crossing time, and the spectrum simultaneously exhibits both blackbody and free-free emission components.
texts.text: (ii) 𝜒 > 1, 𝜂 bo > 1, and 𝜁 < 1: The breakout is out of equilibrium, with the duration set by the light-crossing time, and the spectrum simultaneously exhibits both blackbody and free-free emission components.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (iii) 𝜒 > 1, 𝜂 bo > 1, and 𝜁 > 1: The breakout is out of equilibrium, with the duration set by the light-crossing time, and the spectrum is a Comptonized free-free spectrum.
texts.text: (iii) 𝜒 > 1, 𝜂 bo > 1, and 𝜁 > 1: The breakout is out of equilibrium, with the duration set by the light-crossing time, and the spectrum is a Comptonized free-free spectrum.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (iv) 𝜒 < 1, 𝜂 bo < 1, and 𝜁 = 1: The breakout is in equilibrium, with the duration set by the diffusion time of the breakout layer, and the spectrum is a blackbody.
texts.text: (iv) 𝜒 < 1, 𝜂 bo < 1, and 𝜁 = 1: The breakout is in equilibrium, with the duration set by the diffusion time of the breakout layer, and the spectrum is a blackbody.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (v) 𝜒 < 1, 𝜂 bo > 1, and 𝜁 > 1: The breakout is out of equilibrium, with the duration set by the diffusion time of the breakout layer, and the spectrum is a Comptonized free-free spectrum.
texts.text: (v) 𝜒 < 1, 𝜂 bo > 1, and 𝜁 > 1: The breakout is out of equilibrium, with the duration set by the diffusion time of the breakout layer, and the spectrum is a Comptonized free-free spectrum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All 5 regions meet along the line give by equation 33, since 𝜁 = 𝜒 = 𝜂 bo = 1 there. Regions I and IV (thermal breakout), and III and V (NEST breakout), were realized before and have been discussed by previous authors (e.g., NS10, FS19, Katz, Budnik, & Waxman 2010). However, region II, which represents the INERT breakout case, has not been explored until now.
texts.text: All 5 regions meet along the line give by equation 33, since 𝜁 = 𝜒 = 𝜂 bo = 1 there. Regions I and IV (thermal breakout), and III and V (NEST breakout), were realized before and have been discussed by previous authors (e.g., NS10, FS19, Katz, Budnik, & Waxman 2010). However, region II, which represents the INERT breakout case, has not been explored until now.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To give an idea of the general behaviour, we plot several 2D slices of the 4D ( 𝑛, 𝑅 bo , 𝜌 bo , 𝑣 bo ) parameter space in Fig. 3. In each panel, 𝜌 bo varies from 10 -12 g cm -3 to 10 -7 g cm -3 ; 𝑣 bo varies from 0 . 01 𝑐 to 0 . 2 𝑐 ; 𝑛 is fixed to 0, 1.5, or 3; and 𝑅 bo is fixed to 5, 50, 500, or 5000R ⊙ . The reason for considering the non-standard 𝑛 = 0 case will become clear later, in Section 3.3. As noted in Section 2.1, for temperatures 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo > ∼ 50 keV, pair production becomes important (e.g., NS10, FS19, Katz, Budnik, & Waxman 2010; Faran & Sari 2023), and our model, which neglects pairs, breaks down. For this reason, our model is invalid within the hatched region in the figure, which roughly corresponds to 𝑣 bo > ∼ 0 . 3 𝑐 (the relation between 𝑣 bo and 𝑇 obs , bo is derived in Section 4.4). In addition, as discussed in Section 2.1, our model only applies when 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < 𝑅 bo . Since 𝑡 bo ∝ ( 𝜌 bo 𝑣 2 bo ) -1 according to equation 1, this condition becomes relevant for low densities, slow velocities, or small radii, as shown by the grey region in the figure.

In each panel, 𝜌 bo varies from 10 -12 g cm -3 to 10 -7 g cm -3 ; 𝑣 bo varies from 0 . 01 𝑐 to 0 . 2 𝑐 ; 𝑛 is fixed to 0, 1.5, or 3; and 𝑅 bo is fixed to 5, 50, 500, or 5000R ⊙ . The reason for considering the non-standard 𝑛 = 0 case will become clear later, in Section 3.3. As noted in Section 2.1, for temperatures 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo > ∼ 50 keV, pair production becomes important (e.g., NS10, FS19, Katz, Budnik, & Waxman 2010; Faran & Sari 2023), and our model, which neglects pairs, breaks down. For this reason, our model is invalid within the hatched region in the figure, which roughly corresponds to 𝑣 bo > ∼ 0 . 3 𝑐 (the relation between 𝑣 bo and 𝑇 obs , bo is derived in Section 4.4). In addition, as discussed in Section 2.1, our model only applies when 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < 𝑅 bo . Since 𝑡 bo ∝ ( 𝜌 bo 𝑣 2 bo ) -1 according to equation 1, this condition becomes relevant for low densities, slow velocities, or small radii, as shown by the grey region in the figure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We highlight four panels in particular: 𝑅 bo = 5R ⊙ and 𝑛 = 3, corresponding to a typical Wolf-Rayet star; 𝑅 bo = 50R ⊙ and 𝑛 = 3, corresponding to a typical blue supergiant; 𝑅 bo = 500R ⊙ and 𝑛 = 1 . 5, corresponding to a typical red supergiant; and 𝑅 bo = 5000R ⊙ and 𝑛 = 1 . 5, corresponding to a star with an extended envelope supported by convection. In each of these cases we mark the typical expected values of 𝜌 bo and 𝑣 bo with a star (for the extended envelope case, the typical values are less clear, but we adopt 𝜌 bo = 3 × 10 -11 g cm -3 and 𝑣 bo = 0 . 08 𝑐 as an illustrative example).

In each of these cases we mark the typical expected values of 𝜌 bo and 𝑣 bo with a star (for the extended envelope case, the typical values are less clear, but we adopt 𝜌 bo = 3 × 10 -11 g cm -3 and 𝑣 bo = 0 . 08 𝑐 as an illustrative example).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We see that for red supergiants, 𝜂 bo ≪ 1, 𝜒 ≈ 1, and 𝜁 ≈ 1. As expected, we find that a red supergiant shock breakout is situated near the boundary of regions I and IV, meaning that the breakout is deep in the blackbody regime, and light-crossing and diffusion effects are comparable. For Wolf-Rayet stars, 𝜂 bo ≫ 1, 𝜒 ≫ 1 and 𝜁 ≈ 5, placing the breakout in region III. Thus, a Wolf-Rayet shock breakout is deep in the free-free regime, and the duration is set by the light-crossing time rather than the diffusion time.
texts.text: We see that for red supergiants, 𝜂 bo ≪ 1, 𝜒 ≈ 1, and 𝜁 ≈ 1. As expected, we find that a red supergiant shock breakout is situated near the boundary of regions I and IV, meaning that the breakout is deep in the blackbody regime, and light-crossing and diffusion effects are comparable. For Wolf-Rayet stars, 𝜂 bo ≫ 1, 𝜒 ≫ 1 and 𝜁 ≈ 5, placing the breakout in region III. Thus, a Wolf-Rayet shock breakout is deep in the free-free regime, and the duration is set by the light-crossing time rather than the diffusion time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So far, these results agree with previous studies (e.g, NS10, FS19). However, interestingly, we find that in the blue supergiant case, 𝜂 bo ≈ 5, 𝜒 ≈ 2, and 𝜁 ≈ 1 . 4. This places a blue supergiant shock breakout marginally within region III, but very close to the boundary of region II, the INERT regime. We conclude that shock breakouts from the surface of blue supergiants might sometimes show blackbody and free-free emission simultaneously. The shock breakout spectrum and light curve in the blue supergiant case might therefore differ from what was calculated in previous studies like NS10 and FS19, which did not consider the possibility that the equilibrium time could be shorter than the breakout duration. Updated modelling of blue supergiant shock breakout is beyond the scope of this paper, but will be considered in future work.

texts.orig: Figure 3. The 𝑣 bo versus 𝜌 bo parameter space for 𝑛 = ( 0 , 1 . 5 , 3 ) (columns, left to right) and 𝑅 bo = ( 5 , 50 , 500 , 5000 ) R ⊙ (rows, top to bottom). All panels have the same limits for both axes. Colour indicates the value of 𝜁 (red for 𝜁 ≪ 1, yellow for 𝜁 ∼ 1, and blue for 𝜁 ≫ 1; values greater than 10 5 or less than 10 -5 are solid blue or red, respectively). Lines indicate the conditions 𝜒 = 1 (solid), which separates diffusion-dominated and light-travel dominated breakouts; 𝜂 bo = 1 (dashed), which separates thermal and non-thermal breakouts; and 𝜁 = 1 (dot-dashed), which determines whether or not thermalization occurs within a light-crossing time. The pair creation threshold 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo > ∼ 50 keV, as well as the relativistic shock breakout condition 𝑣 bo > ∼ 0 . 6 𝑐 suggested by Katz, Budnik, & Waxman (2010) and Faran & Sari (2023), are also shown as heavy solid and dotted lines, respectively (as labelled in the bottom left panel). The diagonal hatching indicates where our model breaks down due to pair creation. In the grey region, the breakout takes place far from the edge of the system and our assumption of 𝑅 bo ≈ 𝑅 is invalid. The line 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo = 𝑅 bo , which marks the boundary of the gray region, is labelled in the 𝑛 = 1 . 5 and 𝑅 = 50 R ⊙ panel. In that same panel, we have also labelled the regions I-V corresponding to the five types of breakout discussed in the text. The magenta stars show estimated values for several progenitors: a red supergiant (RSG), a blue supergiant (BSG), a Wolf-Rayet star (WR), and a breakout in an extended convective envelope (EE). All results assume a value of 𝑞 = 1.

texts.orig: To wrap up this section, let us examine the regions in Fig. 3 to see what conclusions can be drawn about favourable conditions for INERT breakout. First, in order to be in region II, 𝑣 bo and 𝜌 bo must be larger than some minimum values. The minimum value occurs at the point where all five regions meet, which is located along the line described by equation 33. The minimum breakout velocity is ≈ 0 . 04 𝑐 , with an extremely weak dependence on the breakout radius ( 𝑣 bo ∝ 𝑅 -1 / 32 bo via equation 33), and only a slight dependence on 𝑛 . The minimum breakout density depends mostly on 𝑅 bo , as 𝜌 bo , -11 > ∼ 0 . 4 𝑅 -15 / 16 bo , 14 , with a weak dependence on 𝑛 . The breakout velocity also has a maximum value, since if the shock is too fast the breakout will be be in region III instead of region II. The maximum velocity has a non-trivial dependence on 𝜌 bo , 𝑅 bo , and 𝑛 , but in general it lies somewhere in the range of 0 . 04-0 . 2 𝑐 . We point out that a velocity estimate of 0 . 1 𝑐 for 𝑙𝑙 GRBs (as mentioned in Section 2.1, see also Paper III) is consistent with the typical range in which INERT breakout is relevant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, provided that 0 . 04 𝑐 < ∼ 𝑣 bo < ∼ 0 . 2 𝑐 , the breakout will always be in region II for a sufficiently high 𝜌 bo , so increasing the density is almost always favourable for obtaining an INERT breakout. For a fixed breakout density, a larger value of 𝑅 bo is favourable because it lowers the minimum density required (however, see Section 3.2). Increasing 𝑛 also seems favourable, because it increases the maximum allowed value of 𝑣 bo (however, again, see Section 3.2).

texts.orig: 3.2 Shock breakout from an extended envelope
texts.text: 3.2 Shock breakout from an extended envelope

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the discussion in Section 3.1, it seems that shock breakout in an extended low-mass envelope is a promising scenario for producing an INERT breakout. We will now consider the behaviour in that case. We suppose that an energy 𝐸 0 is deposited in an envelope of mass 𝑀 env and radius 𝑅 env, with a density profile 𝜌 ∝ ( 𝑅 env -𝑟 ) 𝑛 in the outer layers of the envelope. In order to translate the results of Section 3.1 to these parameters, we need to express 𝜌 bo and 𝑣 bo in terms of 𝐸 0 , 𝑀 env, 𝑅 env and 𝑛 . To accomplish this, we must first specify the density profile.

texts.orig: and 𝑅 𝑡 = 𝑅 env /( 1 + 𝑛 / 𝑠 ) . In this picture, for fixed 𝑠 , the value of 𝑛 determines both the steepness and the thickness of the outer density profile. If 𝑛 > ∼ 𝑠 , then the outer density profile is thick, with Λ ∼ 1. On the other hand, if 𝑛 ≪ 𝑠 , the outer density profile is thin, with Λ ≪ 1. As 𝑛 / 𝑠 → 0, Λ → 0 and as 𝑛 / 𝑠 → ∞ , Λ → 1. Assuming that 𝑠 < 3, so that most of the envelope mass is at large radii, the density 𝜌 env can be estimated by

If 𝑛 > ∼ 𝑠 , then the outer density profile is thick, with Λ ∼ 1. On the other hand, if 𝑛 ≪ 𝑠 , the outer density profile is thin, with Λ ≪ 1. As 𝑛 / 𝑠 → 0, Λ → 0 and as 𝑛 / 𝑠 → ∞ , Λ → 1. texts.text: Here, for simplicity we have approximated the envelope mass by assuming that the 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -𝑠 behaviour continues out to 𝑟 = 𝑅 env. For 𝑠 = 2 and 𝑛 ≤ 3, this overestimates the true envelope mass by at most 50 per cent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The appropriate values of 𝑠 and 𝑛 depend on the structure of the envelope and how it was formed. If the envelope is in hydrostatic equilibrium, with a polytropic equation of state, then 𝑛 will be the polytropic index, and the inner region could be approximated by a power-law with 𝑠 somewhere between 1 and 3. On the other hand, if the envelope was produced by a steady wind, then 𝑠 = 2. In this scenario, 𝑛 would depend on the initial ramping-up of the wind velocity and mass-loss rate, before reaching a steady-state with constant mass-loss rate. If the wind became steady on a short time-scale compared to its age, it could have a sharp edge, corresponding to a small 𝑛 . Another possibly is that the envelope was produced by a stellar eruption or precursor explosion. In this case, the expected profile has an inner region of bound material where 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -3 / 2 and an outer region of unbound ejecta with 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -10 or steeper (e.g., Matzner & McKee 1999; Kuriyama & Shigeyama 2020; Tsuna et al. 2021; Ko et al. 2022). This situation differs from our assumptions in that the density does not go to zero at a finite radius, and the velocity in the steep region may not be well described by the plane-parallel solution of Sakurai (1960). However, for such a steep outer density gradient, the density changes over scales much less than 𝑟 , so in that sense the behaviour might be similar to the Λ ≪ 1 case of our model. Thus, the eruption case might be roughly described by taking 𝑠 = 3 / 2 and 𝑛 ≪ 1.

texts.orig: The quantity Ψ is effectively the ratio of the characteristic optical depth scale of the envelope, 𝜏 env = 𝜅𝜌 env 𝑅 env ( 𝑛 + 1 ) -1 , to 𝑐 / 𝑣 0 . Our assumption that the shock breakout occurs near the edge of the envelope, rather than deep in its interior (i.e., 𝑅 bo ≈ 𝑅 env), corresponds to Ψ > ∼ 1. This requirement is equivalent to the condition 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < 𝑅 bo used in Sections 2.1 and 3.1 when Λ ≈ 1. However, when 𝑛 is small and Λ ≪ 1, the situation is more subtle, as will be addressed in Section 3.3.

texts.orig: As desired, we have obtained expressions linking the breakout parameters to the envelope properties and the injected energy. We can now repeat the procedure of Section 3.1. First, we calculate 𝜒 and 𝜂 bo using equations 43 and 45. Then, as before 𝜁 can be determined using equation 31. The results of carrying out this procedure over a large grid of possible parameter values are shown in Figs. 4 and 5. Fig. 4 shows the dependence on 𝑀 env and 𝑅 env for fixed 𝑛 and 𝐸 0 , while Fig. 5 shows the dependence on 𝑀 env and 𝐸 0 for fixed 𝑛 and 𝑅 env. As discussed above, our model is valid only for Ψ > ∼ 1, which holds for large masses, small radii, and large energies. This condition is violated in the grey region of the figure, where Ψ < 1, and it is marginal in the region with dotted hatching, where 1 ≤ Ψ ≤ 3.

The boundaries of these regions are also labelled in the middle left panel. All results assume a value of 𝑞 = 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As in Fig. 3, we see that the parameter space is divided into five regions, which all meet at a single point. Another similarity is that there is a preferred range of velocities to achieve an INERT breakout. This can be seen in Fig. 5: since 𝑣 0 ∝ 𝐸 1 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env , and the scaling of 𝑣 bo is almost the same (ranging from 𝑣 bo ∝ 𝐸 1 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env for 𝑛 = 0 to 𝐸 0 . 58 0 𝑀 -0 . 42 env for 𝑛 = 3), lines of constant 𝑣 bo approximately follow 𝐸 0 ∝ 𝑀 env. As an example we include several such constant𝑣 bo lines in Fig. 5. We see from the figure that if the energy is large enough and the breakout velocity is close to a certain value (typically 5-10 per cent of 𝑐 ), then we almost always expect to obtain an INERT breakout. This feature arises due to the fact that 𝜂 bo depends very strongly on 𝑣 bo , but only weakly on 𝜌 bo , as seen in equation 2. Consequently, surfaces of constant 𝑣 bo will always be nearly parallel to surfaces of constant 𝜂 bo regardless of the chosen parametrization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Atthe same time, there are several key differences when comparing Figs. 4 and 5 to the results of Section 3.1. First, whereas the value of 𝑛 played only a minor role in Fig. 3, in Figs. 4 and 5 the results significantly depend on 𝑛 . For 𝑛 = 0, a substantial part of parameter space results in INERT breakout. For 𝑛 = 1 . 5, INERT breakout is only possible for a small envelope radius < ∼ 3 × 10 13 cm, and an envelope mass of at least 10 -2 M ⊙ . For 𝑛 = 3, over the range of values we consider, there is hardly any region of parameter space where an INERT breakout can occur. Moreover, whereas in in Fig. 3 the size of region II increased with increasing breakout radius, in Figs. 4 and 5 we find that region II is only present for sufficiently small 𝑅 env. The maximum allowable 𝑅 env is decreased for larger 𝑛 and smaller 𝐸 0 .
texts.text: Atthe same time, there are several key differences when comparing Figs. 4 and 5 to the results of Section 3.1. First, whereas the value of 𝑛 played only a minor role in Fig. 3, in Figs. 4 and 5 the results significantly depend on 𝑛 . For 𝑛 = 0, a substantial part of parameter space results in INERT breakout. For 𝑛 = 1 . 5, INERT breakout is only possible for a small envelope radius < ∼ 3 × 10 13 cm, and an envelope mass of at least 10 -2 M ⊙ . For 𝑛 = 3, over the range of values we consider, there is hardly any region of parameter space where an INERT breakout can occur. Moreover, whereas in in Fig. 3 the size of region II increased with increasing breakout radius, in Figs. 4 and 5 we find that region II is only present for sufficiently small 𝑅 env. The maximum allowable 𝑅 env is decreased for larger 𝑛 and smaller 𝐸 0 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The fundamental reason for these differences is that in Section 3.1, we varied 𝑅 bo and 𝑛 while treating 𝜌 bo and 𝑣 bo as independent parameters. But in this section, we change 𝜌 bo and 𝑣 bo according to equations 39 and 41 as 𝑅 env and 𝑛 vary. In this picture, both 𝑣 bo and 𝜌 bo depend strongly on 𝑛 , because larger 𝑛 leads to stronger shock acceleration, which significantly increases 𝑣 bo . When 𝑣 bo is large, the breakout occurs at a smaller optical depth 𝑐 / 𝑣 bo , so the breakout density 𝜌 bo is reduced as well. In fact, when 𝑛 is increased, the change in 𝜌 bo is much more significant than the change in 𝑣 bo , as can be seen by comparing the exponents in equations 39 and 41. While 𝑣 bo does not depend much on 𝑅 env ( 𝑣 bo ∝ 𝑅 0 env for 𝑛 = 0 and 𝑣 bo ∝ 𝑅 -0 . 33 env for 𝑛 = 3), 𝜌 bo does depend strongly on 𝑅 env ( 𝜌 bo ∝ 𝑅 -3 env for 𝑛 = 0 and 𝜌 bo ∝ 𝑅 -1 . 25 env for 𝑛 = 3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The differences between this and the previous section can in fact be understood purely from this strong dependence of 𝜌 bo on 𝑅 env and 𝑛 . As we saw in Section 3.1, in order to achieve an INERT breakout, 𝜌 bo must be above some minimum value depending on 𝑅 bo . In the envelope breakout case, 𝑅 bo ≈ 𝑅 env so this minimum value scales as 𝜌 bo , min ∝ 𝑅 -15 / 16 env . Suppose for some value of 𝑅 env and 𝑛 this condition is met and the breakout is in region II. What will happen if we then increase 𝑅 env or 𝑛 ? If 𝑅 env is increased, then as discussed above, for 𝑛 ≤ 3 the value of 𝜌 bo will decrease at least as fast as ∝ 𝑅 -1 . 25 env . That is to say, 𝜌 bo drops off faster than 𝜌 bo , min , so at large enough 𝑅 env the condition 𝜌 bo > 𝜌 bo , min will no longer be possible to satisfy. This is why there is an upper limit on the value of 𝑅 env which can produce an INERT breakout in Fig. 4. Similarly, if 𝑛 is increased instead of 𝑅 env, this will also decrease 𝜌 bo and make the minimum density condition harder to satisfy. To compensate the decrease in 𝜌 bo from increasing 𝑛 , we would have to slightly decrease 𝑅 env, or greatly increase 𝑀 env. This is why region II moves up and to the left in Fig. 4 when 𝑛 is increased. Finally, if 𝑀 env is increased to offset the change in 𝑛 , we also need to change 𝐸 0 roughly in proportion to 𝑀 env, or else 𝑣 bo will also change and we will no longer be in region II. This is why region II moves up and to the right, roughly along a line of constant velocity, as 𝑛 is increased in Fig. 5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, for a realistic extended envelope breakout model, the ideal conditions to achieve an INERT breakout are that 𝑛 should be as small as possible ( 𝑛 = 0 is the best case), 𝑅 env should be less than some maximum value which increases with 𝐸 0 and decreases with 𝑛 , and 𝐸 0 / 𝑀 env should lie in a relatively narrow range to ensure 𝑣 bo ∼ 0 . 1. In light of these findings, we will further investigate the behaviour in the 𝑛 → 0 limit in the next section.
texts.text: In summary, for a realistic extended envelope breakout model, the ideal conditions to achieve an INERT breakout are that 𝑛 should be as small as possible ( 𝑛 = 0 is the best case), 𝑅 env should be less than some maximum value which increases with 𝐸 0 and decreases with 𝑛 , and 𝐸 0 / 𝑀 env should lie in a relatively narrow range to ensure 𝑣 bo ∼ 0 . 1. In light of these findings, we will further investigate the behaviour in the 𝑛 → 0 limit in the next section.

texts.orig: 3.3 The 𝑛 → 0 limit
texts.text: 3.3 The 𝑛 → 0 limit

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Fig. 4, we see that when 𝑛 = 0, an INERT breakout is expected for 𝑀 env ∼ 10 -2 -10 -1 M ⊙ and 𝑅 env < ∼ 10 14 cm. Interestingly, these values are similar to the values of 𝑀 env ∼ 10 -2 M ⊙ and 𝑅 env ∼ 10 13 -10 14 cm inferred from modelling the early optical peak in double-peaked SNe (e.g., Nakar & Piro 2014). This connection is especially interesting in the context of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs, which are also thought to have extended envelopes with similar properties (e.g., Nakar 2015; Irwin & Chevalier 2016), and which are known to exhibit both thermal and non-thermal components in their spectra as discussed in Section 1. We discuss the potential connection to 𝑙𝑙 GRBs more in Paper III. Here, we simply point out that the 𝑛 = 0 case may be relevant to observations, so it is worthwhile to develop a better physical intuition for the non-standard case with small 𝑛 .

Thus the term involving ΛΨ simply goes to unity as its exponent trends to zero, and we obtain

texts.orig: As discussed in Section 3.2, in our model the 𝑛 ≪ 𝑠 case corresponds to a situation in which the steep outer part of the density profile, where 𝜌 ∝ ( 𝑅 env -𝑟 ) 𝑛 , is confined to a geometrically thin layer at the edge of the medium. In other words, the envelope has a sharp boundary where the density goes from 𝜌 env to 0 over a scale much smaller than 𝑅 env. We will refer to this region as the 'acceleration layer,' since it is where shock acceleration primarily takes place. In our model, the thickness of this layer is approximately Λ 𝑅 env. In the 𝑛 = 0 case where Λ → 0, the density jumps from 𝜌 env to 0 discontinuously at 𝑟 = 𝑅 env, i.e. there is no acceleration layer and no shock acceleration. While this may be unphysical (realistically a transition layer, however thin, should be expected), we will argue that with regards to the shock breakout, for sufficiently small 𝑛 the behaviour is the same as if 𝑛 were 0. This is because as 𝑛 decreases, the acceleration layer grows thinner and thinner, and the optical depth through it also decreases accordingly. When 𝑛 becomes small enough
texts.text: As discussed in Section 3.2, in our model the 𝑛 ≪ 𝑠 case corresponds to a situation in which the steep outer part of the density profile, where 𝜌 ∝ ( 𝑅 env -𝑟 ) 𝑛 , is confined to a geometrically thin layer at the edge of the medium. In other words, the envelope has a sharp boundary where the density goes from 𝜌 env to 0 over a scale much smaller than 𝑅 env. We will refer to this region as the 'acceleration layer,' since it is where shock acceleration primarily takes place. In our model, the thickness of this layer is approximately Λ 𝑅 env. In the 𝑛 = 0 case where Λ → 0, the density jumps from 𝜌 env to 0 discontinuously at 𝑟 = 𝑅 env, i.e. there is no acceleration layer and no shock acceleration. While this may be unphysical (realistically a transition layer, however thin, should be expected), we will argue that with regards to the shock breakout, for sufficiently small 𝑛 the behaviour is the same as if 𝑛 were 0. This is because as 𝑛 decreases, the acceleration layer grows thinner and thinner, and the optical depth through it also decreases accordingly. When 𝑛 becomes small enough

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (roughly when 𝑛 / 𝑠 < ∼ Ψ -1 ), the optical depth in the acceleration layer no longer satisfies 𝜏 > 𝑐 / 𝑣 0 , so the breakout must take place before the shock reaches the acceleration layer . In this scenario, the acceleration layer can only influence the breakout through its negligible contribution to the optical depth, so the properties of the breakout should not depend on 𝑛 . Instead, they depend on the conditions just inside of the acceleration layer, where regardless of 𝑛 the shock velocity is ≈ 𝑣 0 and the density is ≈ 𝜌 env. But this situation, where the breakout takes place in a thin shell near the edge of the inner density profile and the shock acceleration is negligible, is exactly the same as what occurs in the 𝑛 = 0 case, where there is no acceleration layer at all.
texts.text: (roughly when 𝑛 / 𝑠 < ∼ Ψ -1 ), the optical depth in the acceleration layer no longer satisfies 𝜏 > 𝑐 / 𝑣 0 , so the breakout must take place before the shock reaches the acceleration layer . In this scenario, the acceleration layer can only influence the breakout through its negligible contribution to the optical depth, so the properties of the breakout should not depend on 𝑛 . Instead, they depend on the conditions just inside of the acceleration layer, where regardless of 𝑛 the shock velocity is ≈ 𝑣 0 and the density is ≈ 𝜌 env. But this situation, where the breakout takes place in a thin shell near the edge of the inner density profile and the shock acceleration is negligible, is exactly the same as what occurs in the 𝑛 = 0 case, where there is no acceleration layer at all.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another way to put it is that the limit 𝑛 → 0 corresponds to a situation where the total optical depth of the envelope is ≫ 𝑐 / 𝑣 bo , but the optical depth of the steep outer part is < ∼ 𝑐 / 𝑣 bo , so that the breakout happens inside of the outer density profile, but still near the edge of the inner density profile. In other words, it is the case where Ψ > ∼ 1, but ΛΨ < ∼ 1. To see this, we can first combine equations 39, 40, and 42 to obtain ΛΨ = ( Λ 𝑅 env / 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo ) 1 + 𝑛 -𝜇𝑛 . If Ψ > Λ -1 , this implies that 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < Λ 𝑅 env, meaning that the breakout takes place in the acceleration layer, as in the standard scenario for breakout from a stellar edge. On the other hand, if Ψ ≈ 1, the breakout takes place inside of the acceleration layer. In this case, we have 𝑣 bo ≈ 𝑣 0 and 𝜌 bo ≈ 𝜌 env, since acceleration is unimportant. Then we see from equations 1 and 40 that Ψ ≈ 1 corresponds to 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo ≈ 𝑅 env. Thus, for 1 < ∼ Ψ < ∼ Λ -1 , we find that Λ 𝑅 env < ∼ 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < ∼ 𝑅 env, so that the breakout shell is thicker than the acceleration layer but still thin compared to 𝑅 env. We stress that this intermediate regime is only relevant if Λ ≪ 1, so that the acceleration layer is confined to a thin shell. When Λ ∼ 1, the breakout is guaranteed to happen in the outer density profile if Ψ ≫ 1.

In other words, it is the case where Ψ > ∼ 1, but ΛΨ < ∼ 1. To see this, we can first combine equations 39, 40, and 42 to obtain ΛΨ = ( Λ 𝑅 env / 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo ) 1 + 𝑛 -𝜇𝑛 . If Ψ > Λ -1 , this implies that 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < Λ 𝑅 env, meaning that the breakout takes place in the acceleration layer, as in the standard scenario for breakout from a stellar edge. On the other hand, if Ψ ≈ 1, the breakout takes place inside of the acceleration layer. In this case, we have 𝑣 bo ≈ 𝑣 0 and 𝜌 bo ≈ 𝜌 env, since acceleration is unimportant. Then we see from equations 1 and 40 that Ψ ≈ 1 corresponds to 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo ≈ 𝑅 env. Thus, for 1 < ∼ Ψ < ∼ Λ -1 , we find that Λ 𝑅 env < ∼ 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < ∼ 𝑅 env, so that the breakout shell is thicker than the acceleration layer but still thin compared to 𝑅 env. We stress that this intermediate regime is only relevant if Λ ≪ 1, so that the acceleration layer is confined to a thin shell. When Λ ∼ 1, the breakout is guaranteed to happen in the outer density profile if Ψ ≫ 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The model presented here can be compared with the recent work of Khatami & Kasen (2024), who also considered how the breakout differs when it occurs in a flat inner density profile versus a steep outer density profile. Their light edge breakout case corresponds to the 𝑛 → ∞ limit in our model, and gives the same scalings when 𝜇 = 0 . 2and Ψ is identified as their parameter 𝜉 . On the other hand, the 𝑛 → 0 limit in our model is similar to the light interior breakout case of their model, where their parameter 𝑘 0 is taken to be small so that the breakout occurs at a radius 𝑅 bo ≈ 𝑅 env. In that case, our scaling for 𝐿 bo agrees with theirs, but we obtain a slightly different scaling for the breakout duration due to a subtlety in how the 𝑡 bo is calculated. The difference is that they approximate 𝑡 bo ≈ 𝑅 bo / 𝑣 bo in their interior breakout case, whereas in our model we use 𝑡 bo ≈ ( 𝑅 env -𝑅 bo )/ 𝑣 bo . After accounting for this change, the two models are brought into agreement. Another difference is that in our model, the steep outer density profile is only finite in extent, and it can be thin compared with 𝑅 env. Because of this, in our model it is possible to achieve the necessary conditions for their interior breakout case even when Ψ > 1, as discussed above.

Their light edge breakout case corresponds to the 𝑛 → ∞ limit in our model, and gives the same scalings when 𝜇 = 0 . 2and Ψ is identified as their parameter 𝜉 . On the other hand, the 𝑛 → 0 limit in our model is similar to the light interior breakout case of their model, where their parameter 𝑘 0 is taken to be small so that the breakout occurs at a radius 𝑅 bo ≈ 𝑅 env. In that case, our scaling for 𝐿 bo agrees with theirs, but we obtain a slightly different scaling for the breakout duration due to a subtlety in how the 𝑡 bo is calculated. The difference is that they approximate 𝑡 bo ≈ 𝑅 bo / 𝑣 bo in their interior breakout case, whereas in our model we use 𝑡 bo ≈ ( 𝑅 env -𝑅 bo )/ 𝑣 bo . After accounting for this change, the two models are brought into agreement. texts.orig: In fact, as we will see in the next section, the 𝑛 = 0 case is also deeply connected to previous work on shock breakout in stellar winds, and to the cooling envelope model often used to describe double-peaked SNe.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4 Connections with previous work
texts.text: 3.4 Connections with previous work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The envelope breakout scenario discussed in Sections 3.2 and 3.3, which is based on the standard treatment of NS10, can be compared with other models in the literature involving a shock interacting with optically thick material surrounding the star. Shock breakout from a stellar wind, as has been studied analytically by various authors (e.g., Chevalier & Irwin 2011; Haynie & Piro 2021), offers one point of comparison. As shown by Chevalier & Irwin (2011), there are two possible scenarios, depending on how the radial extent of the wind, 𝑅 w compares with the characteristic diffusion length scale, 𝑅 d . If 𝑅 w > 𝑅 d , the breakout occurs while the the shock is still deep inside the envelope, while if 𝑅 w < 𝑅 d , the breakout occurs when the shock approaches the edge of the wind. For our purposes, the 𝑅 w < 𝑅 d case is relevant.
texts.text: The envelope breakout scenario discussed in Sections 3.2 and 3.3, which is based on the standard treatment of NS10, can be compared with other models in the literature involving a shock interacting with optically thick material surrounding the star. Shock breakout from a stellar wind, as has been studied analytically by various authors (e.g., Chevalier & Irwin 2011; Haynie & Piro 2021), offers one point of comparison. As shown by Chevalier & Irwin (2011), there are two possible scenarios, depending on how the radial extent of the wind, 𝑅 w compares with the characteristic diffusion length scale, 𝑅 d . If 𝑅 w > 𝑅 d , the breakout occurs while the the shock is still deep inside the envelope, while if 𝑅 w < 𝑅 d , the breakout occurs when the shock approaches the edge of the wind. For our purposes, the 𝑅 w < 𝑅 d case is relevant.

texts.orig: An unexplored regime of shock breakout
texts.text: An unexplored regime of shock breakout

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another point of comparison is the cooling envelope model of Nakar & Piro (2014), which describes the early optical peak in double-peaked Type IIb or Ibc SNe. Their picture involves an extended stellar envelope heated by the passage of a shock, which expands adiabatically and eventually cools once the radiation trapped within can escape.
texts.text: Another point of comparison is the cooling envelope model of Nakar & Piro (2014), which describes the early optical peak in double-peaked Type IIb or Ibc SNe. Their picture involves an extended stellar envelope heated by the passage of a shock, which expands adiabatically and eventually cools once the radiation trapped within can escape.

texts.orig: Here, we will show that these two seemingly distinct models can actually be unified. In fact, both can be viewed as special cases of the envelope shock breakout model presented here, in the limit 𝑛 → 0 where, as discussed above, shock acceleration is not very important.
texts.text: Here, we will show that these two seemingly distinct models can actually be unified. In fact, both can be viewed as special cases of the envelope shock breakout model presented here, in the limit 𝑛 → 0 where, as discussed above, shock acceleration is not very important.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, let us consider the shock breakout emission. From equations 49 and 51, we have 𝑡 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 3 env 𝐸 -1 0 and 𝐿 bo ∝ 𝑅 -1 env 𝐸 3 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env for the 𝑛 = 0 case. This gives a total radiated energy of 𝐸 bo ∝ 𝑡 bo 𝐿 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 2 env 𝐸 1 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env . In Appendix B, we verify that these scalings for the observables are identical to the ones given by Chevalier & Irwin (2011) and Haynie & Piro (2021), although they parametrized the problem differently. Thus, we see that in terms of the duration, luminosity, and radiated energy of the shock breakout, the model considered here, when taking the limit 𝑛 → 0, is effectively equivalent to the 𝑅 w < 𝑅 d case of shock breakout from a stellar wind.
