texts.orig: Now, let us consider the cooling emission. According to NS10, following the breakout, the luminosity first decays as 𝑡 -4 / 3 for 𝑡 < 𝑡 s ≈ 𝑅 env / 𝑣 bo . Then, for 𝑡 > 𝑡 s , the luminosity scales as 𝐿 ∝ 𝑡 ( 2 -2 . 28 𝑛 )/ 3 ( 1 + 1 . 19 𝑛 ) as the luminosity shell recedes into the breakout ejecta. 7 Interestingly, we see that when 𝑛 ≤ 0 . 87, the resulting light curve actually rises again after the initial breakout emission decays. texts.text: Again, we confirm in Appendix B that equations 54-56 are identical to the expressions in Nakar & Piro (2014) up to order-unity factors. Therefore, at late times we find that our model with 𝑛 = 0 indeed reduces to the cooling envelope model of Nakar & Piro (2014).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To summarize, we have introduced a model which is based on NS10, but generalizes their model to the case where the usual 𝜌 ∝ ( 𝑅 -𝑟 ) 𝑛 behaviour smoothly connects to a power-law behaviour 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -𝑠 at smaller radii. In our setup we allow the thickness of the acceleration layer to decrease with 𝑛 in such a way that the system is well-behaved even for 𝑛 = 0. For typical order-unity values of 𝑛 , we recover the results of NS10. When taking 𝑛 → 0, however, we instead obtain the results for breakout from the edge of a stellar wind, as in Chevalier & Irwin (2011) and Haynie & Piro (2021). Moreover, the late-time behaviour of our model when 𝑛 = 0 also captures the cooling envelope model for double-peaked SNe devised by Nakar & Piro (2014). The model we present here can therefore be viewed as a generalized model for shock breakout from the edge of a region, which unifies these seemingly unconnected models in the literature into a single framework.
texts.text: To summarize, we have introduced a model which is based on NS10, but generalizes their model to the case where the usual 𝜌 ∝ ( 𝑅 -𝑟 ) 𝑛 behaviour smoothly connects to a power-law behaviour 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -𝑠 at smaller radii. In our setup we allow the thickness of the acceleration layer to decrease with 𝑛 in such a way that the system is well-behaved even for 𝑛 = 0. For typical order-unity values of 𝑛 , we recover the results of NS10. When taking 𝑛 → 0, however, we instead obtain the results for breakout from the edge of a stellar wind, as in Chevalier & Irwin (2011) and Haynie & Piro (2021). Moreover, the late-time behaviour of our model when 𝑛 = 0 also captures the cooling envelope model for double-peaked SNe devised by Nakar & Piro (2014). The model we present here can therefore be viewed as a generalized model for shock breakout from the edge of a region, which unifies these seemingly unconnected models in the literature into a single framework.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 SPECTRAL MODELLING
texts.text: 4 SPECTRAL MODELLING

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Having determined the physical conditions that lead to INERT breakout, our aim now is to get a better idea of what the spectrum might look like in this case. To accomplish this, we need to develop a spectral model which takes into account non-equilibrium effects, rapid thermalization, and the smearing of the spectrum due to unequal light arrival times, and that will be the topic of this section. We will first describe the model assumptions in Section 4.1, and then show how to construct the spectrum. Our basic approach will be to first consider the evolution of the spectrum when the light-crossing time is neglected (Section 4.2), and then to carry out an integral over the arrival time to account for the fact that the high-latitude emission arrives with a delay (Section 4.3). For simplicity, we consider here only the case of spherical symmetry, and only the evolution during the planar phase ( 𝑡 < 𝑡 s ), where the shock radius is approximately constant. Additionally, in Sections 4.1-4.3, we adopt 𝑞 = 1.
texts.text: Having determined the physical conditions that lead to INERT breakout, our aim now is to get a better idea of what the spectrum might look like in this case. To accomplish this, we need to develop a spectral model which takes into account non-equilibrium effects, rapid thermalization, and the smearing of the spectrum due to unequal light arrival times, and that will be the topic of this section. We will first describe the model assumptions in Section 4.1, and then show how to construct the spectrum. Our basic approach will be to first consider the evolution of the spectrum when the light-crossing time is neglected (Section 4.2), and then to carry out an integral over the arrival time to account for the fact that the high-latitude emission arrives with a delay (Section 4.3). For simplicity, we consider here only the case of spherical symmetry, and only the evolution during the planar phase ( 𝑡 < 𝑡 s ), where the shock radius is approximately constant. Additionally, in Sections 4.1-4.3, we adopt 𝑞 = 1.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1 Model description
texts.text: 4.1 Model description

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The spectral behaviour is completely determined by six quantities which can be computed via the breakout model discussed in Sections 2 and 3. The first three are the time-scales 𝑡 bo , 𝑡 lc and 𝑡 eq , ls , given respectively by equations 1, 5, and 15. The light-crossing time 𝑡 lc controls the overall duration of the bolometric emission; the breakout time-scale 𝑡 bo is effectively the adiabatic cooling time of the breakout shell, which affects the temperature and luminosity evolution; and the equilibrium time of the luminosity shell 𝑡 eq , ls determines when thermal emission starts to be observed. The next two quantities are the breakout shell's bolometric luminosity, 𝐿 bo (equation 7), and its observed temperature, 𝑇 obs , bo (equation 8). These respectively set the normalization of the bolometric light curve and the peak energy. The last parameter, 𝛼 , is a power-law index given by equation 21, which governs the time dependence of the observed temperature for 𝑡 < 𝑡 eq , ls . Since our breakout model has only four free parameters ( 𝜌 bo , 𝑣 bo , 𝑅 bo , and 𝑛 ), the six quantities determining the spectrum are not independent, and are subject to two additional closure relations which will be derived below.

texts.text: While equation 57 applies in any case, the behaviour of equations 58 and 60 depends on the ordering of 𝑡 bo , 𝑡 eq , ls , and 𝑡 s . As discussed in Section 2.1, our assumption that the shock breaks out near the edge of the system implies 𝑣 bo 𝑡 bo < 𝑅 bo , and therefore 𝑡 s > 𝑡 bo . Then, recalling from Section 2 that the spectrum at 𝑡 < 𝑡 eq , ls is a Comptonized free-free spectrum, and the spectrum at 𝑡 > 𝑡 eq , ls is a blackbody, there are three possible behaviours (see also Paper II):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If 𝑡 eq , ls = 𝑡 bo , then the spectrum is always a blackbody and the observed temperature follows 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -1 / 3 from the beginning.
texts.text: If 𝑡 eq , ls = 𝑡 bo , then the spectrum is always a blackbody and the observed temperature follows 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -1 / 3 from the beginning.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 s , the spectrum is initially a Comptonized freefree spectrum, with 𝑇 obs steeply declining and 𝜈 a steeply increasing with time. As 𝑡 → 𝑡 eq , ls , 𝑘𝑇 obs and ℎ𝜈 a approach 𝑘𝑇 eq , ls , and the spectrum becomes a blackbody. After 𝑡 eq , ls , 𝑇 obs breaks to a shallower 𝑡 -1 / 3 evolution for the rest of the planar phase.
texts.text: If 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 s , the spectrum is initially a Comptonized freefree spectrum, with 𝑇 obs steeply declining and 𝜈 a steeply increasing with time. As 𝑡 → 𝑡 eq , ls , 𝑘𝑇 obs and ℎ𝜈 a approach 𝑘𝑇 eq , ls , and the spectrum becomes a blackbody. After 𝑡 eq , ls , 𝑇 obs breaks to a shallower 𝑡 -1 / 3 evolution for the rest of the planar phase.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If 𝑡 eq , ls > 𝑡 s , the temperature decays steeply throughout the entire planar phase, and the spectrum is a Comptonized free-free spectrum. The system remains out of thermal equilibrium up until 𝑡 = 𝑡 s , with ℎ𝜈 a < 𝑘𝑇 obs , and only thermalizes later, during the spherical phase.
texts.text: If 𝑡 eq , ls > 𝑡 s , the temperature decays steeply throughout the entire planar phase, and the spectrum is a Comptonized free-free spectrum. The system remains out of thermal equilibrium up until 𝑡 = 𝑡 s , with ℎ𝜈 a < 𝑘𝑇 obs , and only thermalizes later, during the spherical phase.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regardless of the early evolution, once the flow enters the spherical phase at 𝑡 = 𝑡 s , the gas and radiation quickly thermalize (if they have not done so already), and within a few 𝑡 s the spectrum becomes a blackbody with a temperature obeying 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -𝛼 sph , where typically 𝛼 sph ≈ 0 . 6 (as described in, e.g., NS10, FS19). Here we are interested only in the early evolution, so we do not discuss the spherical phase further.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2 The spectrum produced at the source
texts.text: 4.2 The spectrum produced at the source

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in the preceding section, for non-equilibrium breakouts the spectrum is expected to evolve from a Comptonized free-free spectrum to a blackbody spectrum. Our goal now is to obtain an explicit expression for the spectral luminosity 𝐿 𝜈 as a function of time, which can describe the behaviour in both regimes.
texts.text: As discussed in the preceding section, for non-equilibrium breakouts the spectrum is expected to evolve from a Comptonized free-free spectrum to a blackbody spectrum. Our goal now is to obtain an explicit expression for the spectral luminosity 𝐿 𝜈 as a function of time, which can describe the behaviour in both regimes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We will start by considering the blackbody regime, in which case the spectrum is a blackbody with a temperature of 𝑇 obs = 𝑇 BB and a luminosity given by equation 62. Noticing that 𝐿 = ∫ ∞ 0 𝐿 𝜈 d 𝜈 and ( 𝑎𝑐 / 4 ) 𝑇 4 BB = π ∫ ∞ 0 𝐵 𝜈 ( 𝑇 BB ) d 𝜈 , we can rearrange equation 62 and then differentiate it with respect to 𝜈 to arrive at
texts.text: We will start by considering the blackbody regime, in which case the spectrum is a blackbody with a temperature of 𝑇 obs = 𝑇 BB and a luminosity given by equation 62. Noticing that 𝐿 = ∫ ∞ 0 𝐿 𝜈 d 𝜈 and ( 𝑎𝑐 / 4 ) 𝑇 4 BB = π ∫ ∞ 0 𝐵 𝜈 ( 𝑇 BB ) d 𝜈 , we can rearrange equation 62 and then differentiate it with respect to 𝜈 to arrive at

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝐿 𝜈 = 16 π 2 𝑅 2 𝜏 -1 𝐵 𝜈 ( 𝑇 obs ) , (70)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where 𝜏 is given by equation 63 (note that 𝜏 is independent of 𝜈 for electron scattering), and we used 𝑇 obs = 𝑇 BB for a blackbody. Regardless of the initial conditions, if equilibrium is achieved during the planar phase, the solution for 𝐿 𝜈 should asymptote to the form in equation 70.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, to handle the Comptonized free-free regime, we will make the following simplifying assumption, which will be justified later, in Section 4.4. Motivated by the lack of a clear Wien peak in the observed spectrum of candidate shock breakout events such as SN 2008D and GRB 060218, we assume that during the free-free phase, the spectrum is only modestly affected by Comptonization, i.e. the Comptonization parameter of the luminosity shell 𝜉 ( 𝑇 obs ) is not too large. In particular, provided that the Comptonization parameter is less than a certain critical value (which will be derived in Section 4.4), the effect of Comptonization is to move the peak energy of the free-free spectrum from 𝑘𝑇 to 4 𝑘𝑇 , with the shape of the spectrum remaining mostly unchanged (see, e.g., Margalit, Quataert, & Ho 2022). In this case, we can crudely approximate the spectrum as a modified free-free spectrum with a cutoff at ≈ 4 𝑘𝑇 obs instead of 𝑘𝑇 obs . This approximation is convenient because it ensures that the peak energy (defined as the energy which maximizes 𝜈𝐹 𝜈 ) does not have a discontinuity at 𝑡 = 𝑡 eq , ls , since 𝜈𝐹 𝜈 peaks at 4 𝑘𝑇 obs for both the Comptonized free-free and blackbody cases.
texts.text: Now, to handle the Comptonized free-free regime, we will make the following simplifying assumption, which will be justified later, in Section 4.4. Motivated by the lack of a clear Wien peak in the observed spectrum of candidate shock breakout events such as SN 2008D and GRB 060218, we assume that during the free-free phase, the spectrum is only modestly affected by Comptonization, i.e. the Comptonization parameter of the luminosity shell 𝜉 ( 𝑇 obs ) is not too large. In particular, provided that the Comptonization parameter is less than a certain critical value (which will be derived in Section 4.4), the effect of Comptonization is to move the peak energy of the free-free spectrum from 𝑘𝑇 to 4 𝑘𝑇 , with the shape of the spectrum remaining mostly unchanged (see, e.g., Margalit, Quataert, & Ho 2022). In this case, we can crudely approximate the spectrum as a modified free-free spectrum with a cutoff at ≈ 4 𝑘𝑇 obs instead of 𝑘𝑇 obs . This approximation is convenient because it ensures that the peak energy (defined as the energy which maximizes 𝜈𝐹 𝜈 ) does not have a discontinuity at 𝑡 = 𝑡 eq , ls , since 𝜈𝐹 𝜈 peaks at 4 𝑘𝑇 obs for both the Comptonized free-free and blackbody cases.

texts.orig: The dimensionless quantities 𝐶 𝐿 ( 𝑡 ) and 𝐶 𝜏 ( 𝑡 ) are to be determined below. The coefficient of 𝐼 𝜈 in equation 71 was chosen to ensure that 𝐿 𝜈 takes the form of 70 in the limit 𝜏𝜏 𝜈, ff ≫ 1. Note that equation 73 differs from the standard prescription for free-free absorption due to the presence of a factor of 3.92 in the exponent. This factor is found by maximizing 𝜈𝐵 𝜈 and it is introduced to ensure that throughout the whole evolution, 𝐼 𝜈 always peaks at the same value of ℎ𝜈 / 𝑘𝑇 obs ≈ 3 . 92. By including this factor, the peak of the free-free spectrum is shifted to ≈ 4 𝑘𝑇 obs , which roughly accounts for the effect of Comptonization as discussed above. Although this prescription has a non-standard behaviour at ℎ𝜈 ≫ 𝑘𝑇 obs (i.e., 𝐼 𝜈 → 𝐵 𝜈 instead of 𝐼 𝜈 → 𝜏 𝜈, ff 𝐵 𝜈 as 𝜈 → ∞ ), for our purposes we are not interested in frequencies above the peak, so this difference does not affect our results.

texts.orig: The simplest way to see this is to observe that, by definition, 𝜈 a is the frequency satisfying 𝜏𝜏 𝜈, ff ( 𝜈 a ) = 1. Therefore 𝜏 𝜈, ff ( 𝜈 a ) = 𝜏 -1 and equation 73 leads immediately to equation 74. It is desirable for 𝐶 𝜏 to quickly blow up to infinity after 𝑡 eq , ls , so that the spectrum converges to a blackbody at all frequencies. The exact way this is implemented does not make much difference; we do it by letting 𝜈 a continue to grow according to equation 60 even after 𝑡 eq , ls .

texts.orig: With 𝐶 𝜏 known, 𝐿 𝜈 can be calculated using equations 71-74. The remaining constant 𝐶 𝐿 is then found numerically, by demanding that ∫ ∞ 0 𝐿 𝜈 d 𝜈 = 𝐿 ( 𝑡 ) at all times. By comparing equations 70 and 71, we see that 𝐶 𝐿 → 1 for 𝑡 ≫ 𝑡 eq , ls . Our choice of the coefficient in equation 61 ensures that 𝐶 𝐿 → 1 as 𝑡 → 𝑡 bo as well. In general 𝐶 𝐿 ∼ 1 and it peaks when 𝑡 is an appreciable fraction of 𝑡 eq , ls . As a final consistency check, we confirm that when ℎ𝜈 a ≪ 𝑘𝑇 obs , after substituting equations 58, 60, and 63 into equations 71-74, 𝐿 𝜈 reduces to the form 𝐿 𝜈 ( 𝑡 ) ≈ [ 𝐿 ( 𝑡 )/ 3 . 92 𝑘𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 )] 𝑒 -ℎ𝜈 / 3 . 92 𝑘𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 ) , as expected for free-free emission with luminosity 𝐿 and peak energy 3 . 92 𝑘𝑇 obs .

By comparing equations 70 and 71, we see that 𝐶 𝐿 → 1 for 𝑡 ≫ 𝑡 eq , ls . Our choice of the coefficient in equation 61 ensures that 𝐶 𝐿 → 1 as 𝑡 → 𝑡 bo as well. In general 𝐶 𝐿 ∼ 1 and it peaks when 𝑡 is an appreciable fraction of 𝑡 eq , ls . As a final consistency check, we confirm that when ℎ𝜈 a ≪ 𝑘𝑇 obs , after substituting equations 58, 60, and 63 into equations 71-74, 𝐿 𝜈 reduces to the form 𝐿 𝜈 ( 𝑡 ) ≈ [ 𝐿 ( 𝑡 )/ 3 . 92 𝑘𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 )] 𝑒 -ℎ𝜈 / 3 . 92 𝑘𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 ) , as expected for free-free emission with luminosity 𝐿 and peak energy 3 . 92 𝑘𝑇 obs .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We stress that equation 72 is only an empirical relation that smoothly interpolates between the correct asymptotic behaviours at 𝜈 ≪ 𝜈 a and 𝜈 ≫ 𝜈 a , and is not a solution to the radiative transfer equation in a scattering-dominated medium. Therefore, the actual behaviour of the spectrum in the neighbourhood of 𝜈 ∼ 𝜈 a may differ from what equation 72 predicts. In particular, for large values of the electron scattering optical depth 𝜏 , it is known that the spectrum is altered due to 'modified blackbody' effects, as discussed by Felten & Rees (1972), Illarionov & Sunyaev (1972), and Rybicki & Lightman (1986). In that case, 𝐼 𝜈 remains basically the same at low and high frequencies, but there is an intermediate regime for 𝜈 a / 𝜏 < ∼ 𝜈 < ∼ 𝜈 a where 𝐼 𝜈 ∝ 𝐵 𝜈 𝜏 1 / 2 𝜈, ff ∝ 𝜈 . We investigated also a more general form of equation 72 which accounts for this modification. However, we find that for 𝜏 ∼ 10 as considered in this work, the change in the spectrum is not too significant ( 𝐿 𝜈 changes by < ∼ 50 per cent at all frequencies for 𝜏 = 10). Significant differences become apparent only at 𝜏 > ∼ 30. The basic reason for this is that in the modified blackbody regime, for modest values of 𝜏 the spectrum is affected only over a relatively narrow range of frequencies near 𝜈 a . But, even in the case described by equation 72, the behaviour of the spectrum near 𝜈 a is not so different from 𝐼 𝜈 ∝ 𝜈 anyways, because at that frequency the spectrum is transitioning from the 𝐼 𝜈 ∝ 𝜈 2 Rayleigh-Jeans behaviour to the 𝐼 𝜈 ∝ 𝜈 0 free-free behaviour. However, we caution that for especially slow shocks with large 𝜏 ∼ 𝑐 / 𝑣 , equation 72 may not adequately describe the emergent spectrum, because the 𝐼 𝜈 ∝ 𝜈 behaviour could extend over a much larger frequency range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Wealso checked that the results are not significantly affected when the correct time dependence, including the logarithmic corrections discussed in Section 2.2, is used for 𝜈 a and 𝑇 obs , instead of the approximate power laws assumed in Section 4.1. We find that the overall qualitative picture remains unchanged. The main difference is that, compared to the power-law model, when including the logarithmic terms 𝑇 obs and 𝜈 a evolve slightly faster at times 𝑡 ∼ 𝑡 bo , and slightly slower at times 𝑡 ∼ 𝑡 eq , ls , in such a way that the value of 𝑇 obs ( 𝜈 a ) is always lower (higher) than in the power-law case. As a result, the spectrum for 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 < 𝑡 eq , ls is slightly brighter for ℎ𝜈 a < ℎ𝜈 < 𝑘𝑇 obs , and slightly fainter for 𝜈 < 𝜈 a and ℎ𝜈 > 𝑘𝑇 obs . The behaviour at times 𝑡 > ∼ 𝑡 eq , ls is essentially unchanged due to a weak dependence on the logarithmic term.

texts.orig: To summarize the results of this section, we show an example spectrum computed using our method in Fig. 6. In the figure, we have taken a large value of 𝑡 eq , ls / 𝑡 bo so that both the free-free and blackbody behaviours show up clearly. As expected, we see that the spectral is initially a self-absorbed free-free spectrum. As time goes on, the temperature drops rapidly, while the self-absorption frequency increases. Since we took 𝛼 = 1 . 8 > 4 / 3, the temperature declines faster than the bolometric luminosity, and the peak value of 𝐿 𝜈 increases as 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝐿 / 𝑇 obs up until 𝑡 eq , ls . At this time, the spectrum becomes a blackbody; the temperature continues to decrease, but much more slowly than before. At times 𝑡 > 𝑡 eq , ls , the peak 𝐿 𝜈 gradually fades as 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝐿 / 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -1 .
texts.text: To summarize the results of this section, we show an example spectrum computed using our method in Fig. 6. In the figure, we have taken a large value of 𝑡 eq , ls / 𝑡 bo so that both the free-free and blackbody behaviours show up clearly. As expected, we see that the spectral is initially a self-absorbed free-free spectrum. As time goes on, the temperature drops rapidly, while the self-absorption frequency increases. Since we took 𝛼 = 1 . 8 > 4 / 3, the temperature declines faster than the bolometric luminosity, and the peak value of 𝐿 𝜈 increases as 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝐿 / 𝑇 obs up until 𝑡 eq , ls . At this time, the spectrum becomes a blackbody; the temperature continues to decrease, but much more slowly than before. At times 𝑡 > 𝑡 eq , ls , the peak 𝐿 𝜈 gradually fades as 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝐿 / 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -1 .

texts.text: 4.3 The observed spectrum

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results of the previous section describe the spectrum emitted at the source, which is what an observer would see if the light-crossing time were negligible. Now we need to account for the delay in arrival due to the light travel time. Our approach is similar to that of Irwin et al. (2021). As discussed in Section 2.3, emission from material at an angle of 𝜃 from the line of sight arrives with a delay of 𝑡 del ( 𝜃 ) , with 𝑡 del given by equation 27. The flux arriving from angles between 𝜃 and 𝜃 + d 𝜃 is therefore proportional to 𝐿 𝜈 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 del ( 𝜃 )) . The flux is also proportional to the projected area contained between 𝜃 and 𝜃 + d 𝜃 , which is ∝ sin 𝜃 cos 𝜃 d 𝜃 . The observed luminosity can then be obtained by integrating over 𝜃 :

texts.orig: 2 comes from the fact that 2 ∫ π / 2 0 sin 𝜃 cos 𝜃 d 𝜃 = 1, and ensures that 𝐿 𝜈, obs ≈ 𝐿 𝜈 when the delay time is negligible, i.e. when 𝑡 lc ≪ 𝑡 bo so that the whole surface is revealed in less than the time 𝑡 bo . On the other hand, in the case of significant delay time with 𝑡 lc > ∼ 𝑡 bo , only a small fraction of the surface up to an angle of 𝜃 bo ≈ ( 2 𝑡 bo / 𝑡 lc ) 1 / 2 becomes visible within the time 𝑡 bo (as discussed in Section 2.3), so the initial observed luminosity is reduced according to 𝐿 𝜈, obs ≈ 𝐿 𝜈 𝜃 2 bo ≈ 2 𝐿 𝜈 ( 𝑡 bo / 𝑡 lc ) . This is consistent with the expectation that a non-negligible light-crossing time will not change the total observed energy of the breakout, but will smear out the signal over a time-scale of 𝑡 lc , so that a signal with an intrinsic duration of 𝑡 bo and luminosity of 𝐿 bo will have an observed duration of 𝑡 lc and luminosity of 𝐿 obs ≈ 𝐿 bo ( 𝑡 bo / 𝑡 lc ) after this smearing (see also the discussion surrounding equation 6).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The delayed arrival of the high-latitude emission not only smears the luminosity over a longer time-scale, it also it also smears the single-temperature spectrum into a multi-temperature spectrum (see also Section 2.3). In particular, the spectrum is smeared between a minimum temperature 𝑇 obs , min and a maximum temperature 𝑇 obs , max , as shown in Fig. 7. The minimum observed temperature 𝑇 obs , min is determined by the material along the line of sight, which has been visible since 𝑡 = 0, and it evolves according to equation 58 fromthe beginning, i.e. 𝑇 obs , min = 𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 ) . Material at larger latitudes also evolves according to equation 58, but with a delay, so that the emission originating from angle 𝜃 is observed to have a temperature of 𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 ) = 𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 -𝑡 del ( 𝜃 )) at time 𝑡 . The maximum temperature 𝑇 obs , max is set by the highest-latitude material whose light has had enough time to reach the observer. If 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , this material is located at an angle of 𝜃 ≈ ( 2 𝑡 / 𝑡 lc ) 1 / 2 , where the condition 𝑡 = 𝑡 del ( 𝜃 ) is satisfied. If 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc , it is located at the equator, with 𝜃 = π / 2. Since the temperature of the breakout is the same across the whole surface in our spherical model, 𝑇 obs , max initially stays constant with a value of 𝑇 obs , max = 𝑇 obs ( 0 ) = 𝑇 obs , bo until the whole surface has been revealed at 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc . For 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc , 𝑇 obs , max tracks the temperature of material in the equatorial plane, which evolves according to 𝑇 obs , max = 𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 -𝑡 lc ) to account for its delayed arrival.
texts.text: The delayed arrival of the high-latitude emission not only smears the luminosity over a longer time-scale, it also it also smears the single-temperature spectrum into a multi-temperature spectrum (see also Section 2.3). In particular, the spectrum is smeared between a minimum temperature 𝑇 obs , min and a maximum temperature 𝑇 obs , max , as shown in Fig. 7. The minimum observed temperature 𝑇 obs , min is determined by the material along the line of sight, which has been visible since 𝑡 = 0, and it evolves according to equation 58 fromthe beginning, i.e. 𝑇 obs , min = 𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 ) . Material at larger latitudes also evolves according to equation 58, but with a delay, so that the emission originating from angle 𝜃 is observed to have a temperature of 𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 ) = 𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 -𝑡 del ( 𝜃 )) at time 𝑡 . The maximum temperature 𝑇 obs , max is set by the highest-latitude material whose light has had enough time to reach the observer. If 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , this material is located at an angle of 𝜃 ≈ ( 2 𝑡 / 𝑡 lc ) 1 / 2 , where the condition 𝑡 = 𝑡 del ( 𝜃 ) is satisfied. If 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc , it is located at the equator, with 𝜃 = π / 2. Since the temperature of the breakout is the same across the whole surface in our spherical model, 𝑇 obs , max initially stays constant with a value of 𝑇 obs , max = 𝑇 obs ( 0 ) = 𝑇 obs , bo until the whole surface has been revealed at 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc . For 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc , 𝑇 obs , max tracks the temperature of material in the equatorial plane, which evolves according to 𝑇 obs , max = 𝑇 obs ( 𝑡 -𝑡 lc ) to account for its delayed arrival.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At a given frequency 𝑘𝑇 obs , min < ℎ𝜈 < 𝑘𝑇 obs , max , the ob- served emission is dominated by the emission from the angle satisfying 𝑘𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 ) ∼ ℎ𝜈 . This emission subtends a projected area ∝ 𝜃 2 ; therefore its luminosity is ∝ 𝐿𝜃 2 and its spectral luminosity is 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝐿𝜃 2 / 𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 ) . If 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -𝛼 as in equation 58, then since 𝐿 ∝ 𝑡 -4 / 3 (equation 57) and 𝜃 ∝ 𝑡 1 / 2 (equation 27), we have 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ [ 𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 )] ( 1 -3 𝛼 )/( 3 𝛼 ) ∝ 𝜈 ( 1 -3 𝛼 )/( 3 𝛼 ) for 𝑘𝑇 obs , min < ℎ𝜈 < 𝑘𝑇 obs , max . This power-law scaling agrees with the value obtained by NS10 and FS19, who also considered the behaviour of the spectrum over this frequency range.
texts.text: At a given frequency 𝑘𝑇 obs , min < ℎ𝜈 < 𝑘𝑇 obs , max , the ob- served emission is dominated by the emission from the angle satisfying 𝑘𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 ) ∼ ℎ𝜈 . This emission subtends a projected area ∝ 𝜃 2 ; therefore its luminosity is ∝ 𝐿𝜃 2 and its spectral luminosity is 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝐿𝜃 2 / 𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 ) . If 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -𝛼 as in equation 58, then since 𝐿 ∝ 𝑡 -4 / 3 (equation 57) and 𝜃 ∝ 𝑡 1 / 2 (equation 27), we have 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ [ 𝑇 obs ( 𝜃 )] ( 1 -3 𝛼 )/( 3 𝛼 ) ∝ 𝜈 ( 1 -3 𝛼 )/( 3 𝛼 ) for 𝑘𝑇 obs , min < ℎ𝜈 < 𝑘𝑇 obs , max . This power-law scaling agrees with the value obtained by NS10 and FS19, who also considered the behaviour of the spectrum over this frequency range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However,aneffect that was not discussed by NS10 and FS19 is that, in the free-free spectral regime, the self-absorption frequency is also smeared out in a way analogous to the temperature, so that the spectrum at low frequencies is also altered between frequencies 𝜈 a , min and 𝜈 a , max, as shown in red in Fig. 7. Since 𝜈 a is increasing instead of decreasing with time, in this case it is 𝜈 a , max = 𝜈 a ( 𝑡 ) whichevolves from the beginning, and 𝜈 a , min which initially remains steady at a value of 𝜈 a , max = 𝜈 a , bo for 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , and then evolves as 𝜈 a , max = 𝜈 a ( 𝑡 -𝑡 lc ) for 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc . By an argument identical to the one used above to determine the spectrum between 𝑘𝑇 obs , min and 𝑘𝑇 obs , max , except that we have 𝜈 a ∝ 𝑡 𝜆 ( 𝛼 -2 / 3 ) from equation 60 instead of 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -𝛼 , we obtain 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝜈 ( 3 𝛼 -1 )/[ 𝜆 ( 3 𝛼 -2 ) ] for 𝜈 a , min < 𝜈 < 𝜈 a , max.
texts.text: However,aneffect that was not discussed by NS10 and FS19 is that, in the free-free spectral regime, the self-absorption frequency is also smeared out in a way analogous to the temperature, so that the spectrum at low frequencies is also altered between frequencies 𝜈 a , min and 𝜈 a , max, as shown in red in Fig. 7. Since 𝜈 a is increasing instead of decreasing with time, in this case it is 𝜈 a , max = 𝜈 a ( 𝑡 ) whichevolves from the beginning, and 𝜈 a , min which initially remains steady at a value of 𝜈 a , max = 𝜈 a , bo for 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , and then evolves as 𝜈 a , max = 𝜈 a ( 𝑡 -𝑡 lc ) for 𝑡 > 𝑡 lc . By an argument identical to the one used above to determine the spectrum between 𝑘𝑇 obs , min and 𝑘𝑇 obs , max , except that we have 𝜈 a ∝ 𝑡 𝜆 ( 𝛼 -2 / 3 ) from equation 60 instead of 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -𝛼 , we obtain 𝐿 𝜈 ∝ 𝜈 ( 3 𝛼 -1 )/[ 𝜆 ( 3 𝛼 -2 ) ] for 𝜈 a , min < 𝜈 < 𝜈 a , max.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, as discussed in Section 2.3, we are mainly interested in the case 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 lc (i.e., the INERT breakout case), where the radiation and gas are initially out of equilibrium, smearing by light travel time is important, and blackbody and free-free components are simultaneously present in the spectrum. (For example spectra for other orderings of these time-scales, see Paper II.) Fig. 8 shows an illustrative example of the spectral evolution in this case, for 𝑡 eq , ls = 40 𝑡 bo and 𝑡 lc = 200 𝑡 bo . Here, a large value of 𝑡 eq , ls / 𝑡 bo was adopted so that the spectrum is smeared over a broad frequency range and the expected power-law behaviours are clearly exhibited, and we also took 𝑡 lc / 𝑡 eq , ls to be large so that the smearing effect persists for an extended time. For an example with more realistic parameter values, see Paper III. As expected, we see that the spectrum initially resembles a self-absorbed free-free spectrum. As time goes on, the high-energy spectrum is smeared into a power-law between 𝑇 obs , min and 𝑇 obs , max , and the low-energy spectrum is smeared into a powerlaw between 𝜈 a , min and 𝜈 a , max. We find that the power-law indices are in good agreement with the predictions above, particularly for times 𝑡 > ∼ 𝑡 eq , ls when enough time has passed to smear the spectrum over a few decades in frequency.

(For example spectra for other orderings of these time-scales, see Paper II.) Fig. 8 shows an illustrative example of the spectral evolution in this case, for 𝑡 eq , ls = 40 𝑡 bo and 𝑡 lc = 200 𝑡 bo . Here, a large value of 𝑡 eq , ls / 𝑡 bo was adopted so that the spectrum is smeared over a broad frequency range and the expected power-law behaviours are clearly exhibited, and we also took 𝑡 lc / 𝑡 eq , ls to be large so that the smearing effect persists for an extended time. As time goes on, the high-energy spectrum is smeared into a power-law between 𝑇 obs , min and 𝑇 obs , max , and the low-energy spectrum is smeared into a powerlaw between 𝜈 a , min and 𝜈 a , max. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once 𝑡 = 𝑡 eq , ls , the material along the line of sight with temperature 𝑇 obs , min obtains thermal equilibrium, and a blackbody component appears in the spectrum. As we saw in Section 4.2, the temperature decay becomes much slower upon entering the blackbody regime. Therefore, unlike the free-free emission, the blackbody emission is not significantly smeared by the effects of light travel time. At intermediate times 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , as expected from Section 2.3, both a blackbody component and a smeared out free-free component are present in the spectrum, as is clearly seen for the brown ( 𝑡 = 1 . 9 𝑡 eq , ls ) and pink ( 𝑡 = 4 . 4 𝑡 eq , ls ) lines in Fig. 8. As discussed in Section 2.3, the blackbody emission is produced by material with 𝜃 < ∼ [ 2 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls )/ 𝑡 lc ] 1 / 2 , while the free-free emission comes from material at angles larger than this.
texts.text: Once 𝑡 = 𝑡 eq , ls , the material along the line of sight with temperature 𝑇 obs , min obtains thermal equilibrium, and a blackbody component appears in the spectrum. As we saw in Section 4.2, the temperature decay becomes much slower upon entering the blackbody regime. Therefore, unlike the free-free emission, the blackbody emission is not significantly smeared by the effects of light travel time. At intermediate times 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 < 𝑡 lc , as expected from Section 2.3, both a blackbody component and a smeared out free-free component are present in the spectrum, as is clearly seen for the brown ( 𝑡 = 1 . 9 𝑡 eq , ls ) and pink ( 𝑡 = 4 . 4 𝑡 eq , ls ) lines in Fig. 8. As discussed in Section 2.3, the blackbody emission is produced by material with 𝜃 < ∼ [ 2 ( 𝑡 -𝑡 eq , ls )/ 𝑡 lc ] 1 / 2 , while the free-free emission comes from material at angles larger than this.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As 𝑡 approaches 𝑡 lc , the free-free breakout emission arrives from material closer and closer to the equator. Although this material still emits with a temperature of 𝑇 obs , bo , its luminosity is suppressed by projected area effects (i.e., by the factor of cos 𝜃 appearing in equation 75). The hotter material, which comes from larger 𝜃 , is suppressed more and as a result, the free-free component of the spectrum starts to become fainter. The spectrum grows softer at high energies, and harder at low energies, and it no longer follows the expected power-law behaviours described above. At 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc , the material at 𝜃 = π / 2 is finally seen, and 𝑇 obs , max starts to drop rapidly. Within a few 𝑡 bo after 𝑡 lc , the equatorial material has cooled significantly and the spectrum becomes very soft at high energies. Ultimately, at 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , even the material at the equator thermalizes and the free-free component disappears completely. As expected, the latetime spectrum is simply a blackbody.

Ultimately, at 𝑡 = 𝑡 lc + 𝑡 eq , ls , even the material at the equator thermalizes and the free-free component disappears completely. As expected, the latetime spectrum is simply a blackbody.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4 Comptonization
texts.text: 4.4 Comptonization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, let us consider the effect of Comptonization, and show that our assumption that it does not strongly influence the spectrum is valid. Since 𝜉 ls ( 𝑇 obs , ls ) is a decreasing function of temperature (e.g., NS10), and the temperatures of all shells inside the breakout shell satisfy 𝑇 obs , ls ≤ 𝑇 obs , bo , the Comptonization parameter of the luminosity shell satisfies 𝜉 ls ≤ 𝜉 bo at all times. Moreover, the rapid evolution of 𝑇 obs , ls (as discussed in Section 2.2) will ensure that 𝜉 ls quickly falls below its initial value. Therefore, it is sufficient to show that the Comptonization parameter of the breakout shell, 𝜉 bo , is not too large. Our discussion here mainly follows FS19 and Margalit, Quataert, & Ho (2022) (see also Kompaneets 1957; Illarionov & Sunyaev 1972; Rybicki & Lightman 1986).
texts.text: Finally, let us consider the effect of Comptonization, and show that our assumption that it does not strongly influence the spectrum is valid. Since 𝜉 ls ( 𝑇 obs , ls ) is a decreasing function of temperature (e.g., NS10), and the temperatures of all shells inside the breakout shell satisfy 𝑇 obs , ls ≤ 𝑇 obs , bo , the Comptonization parameter of the luminosity shell satisfies 𝜉 ls ≤ 𝜉 bo at all times. Moreover, the rapid evolution of 𝑇 obs , ls (as discussed in Section 2.2) will ensure that 𝜉 ls quickly falls below its initial value. Therefore, it is sufficient to show that the Comptonization parameter of the breakout shell, 𝜉 bo , is not too large. Our discussion here mainly follows FS19 and Margalit, Quataert, & Ho (2022) (see also Kompaneets 1957; Illarionov & Sunyaev 1972; Rybicki & Lightman 1986).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The role of Comptonization in altering the spectrum is basically to bring low-energy photons with energies ℎ𝜈 ≪ 𝑘𝑇 up to energies of order 4 𝑘𝑇 . This increases the number of photons which are available to share the internal energy, and decreases the temperature. The importance of Comptonization depends on the number of times a typical photon is scattered before escaping the system. Photons which are generated with lower energies require a greater number of scatterings to be brought up to 4 𝑘𝑇 , and are also more susceptible to self-absorption. There is a critical frequency, 𝜈 coh , below which photons are destroyed by self-absorption before they can be Comptonized. If a typical photon undergoes enough scatterings, all photons with 𝜈 > ∼ 𝜈 coh will be brought up to the peak, and the Comptonization will saturate. On the other hand, if not enough scatterings occur, then only photons with frequencies 𝜈 > 𝜈 𝑦 can be brought up to the peak energy, where ℎ𝜈 𝑦 ≈ 𝑘𝑇 obs 𝑒 -𝑦 ( 𝑐 / 𝑣𝜏 ) (e.g., FS19), 𝑦 ≈ ( 4 𝑘𝑇 obs / 𝑚 e 𝑐 2 ) 𝜏 2 is the Compton parameter, and 𝑚 e is the electron mass.

The importance of Comptonization depends on the number of times a typical photon is scattered before escaping the system. Photons which are generated with lower energies require a greater number of scatterings to be brought up to 4 𝑘𝑇 , and are also more susceptible to self-absorption. If a typical photon undergoes enough scatterings, all photons with 𝜈 > ∼ 𝜈 coh will be brought up to the peak, and the Comptonization will saturate. On the other hand, if not enough scatterings occur, then only photons with frequencies 𝜈 > 𝜈 𝑦 can be brought up to the peak energy, where ℎ𝜈 𝑦 ≈ 𝑘𝑇 obs 𝑒 -𝑦 ( 𝑐 / 𝑣𝜏 ) (e.g., FS19), 𝑦 ≈ ( 4 𝑘𝑇 obs / 𝑚 e 𝑐 2 ) 𝜏 2 is the Compton parameter, and 𝑚 e is the electron mass.

texts.orig: The value of 𝑦 bo is very sensitive to 𝑣 bo , and therefore 𝜈 𝑦, bo is poorly constrained in general. For velocities < ∼ 0 . 1 𝑐 , equation 76 predicts 𝑦 bo < ∼ 10. In this case 𝜈 𝑦, bo falls in the range 1-10 4 eV. Comparing with equation 61 then suggests that 𝜈 𝑦, bo > 𝜈 a , bo , indicating that most photons generated in the breakout shell can diffuse out of the system before they can be Comptonized, although care must be taken here because 𝜈 a , bo also depends sensitively on 𝑣 bo .

In this case 𝜈 𝑦, bo falls in the range 1-10 4 eV. Comparing with equation 61 then suggests that 𝜈 𝑦, bo > 𝜈 a , bo , indicating that most photons generated in the breakout shell can diffuse out of the system before they can be Comptonized, although care must be taken here because 𝜈 a , bo also depends sensitively on 𝑣 bo .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is useful to define two critical values of 𝑦 : 𝑦 a for which 𝜈 𝑦 = 𝜈 a , and 𝑦 sat for which 𝜈 𝑦 = 𝜈 coh . For 𝑦 > ∼ 𝑦 a ( 𝜈 𝑦 < 𝜈 a ), the value of 𝜉 saturates because the supply of photons available to Comptonize has effectively been exhausted (e.g., FS19). Since the photon number spectrum peaks at 𝜈 a for self-absorbed free-free emission, the number of photons with 𝜈 < 𝜈 a is negligible. So, even though additional photons from below 𝜈 a can be brought to the peak for 𝑦 > ∼ 𝑦 a , their presence does not appreciably change the photon number density at 𝑘𝑇 obs or the value of 𝜉 . For the breakout shell, a value of 𝑦 a , bo ≈ ln ( 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo / ℎ𝜈 a , bo ) is found by setting 𝜈 𝑦, bo = 𝜈 a , bo in equation 77. Note that 𝑦 a is only defined for out-of-equilibrium systems with 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo > ℎ𝜈 a , bo , and otherwise we set it to 0.
texts.text: It is useful to define two critical values of 𝑦 : 𝑦 a for which 𝜈 𝑦 = 𝜈 a , and 𝑦 sat for which 𝜈 𝑦 = 𝜈 coh . For 𝑦 > ∼ 𝑦 a ( 𝜈 𝑦 < 𝜈 a ), the value of 𝜉 saturates because the supply of photons available to Comptonize has effectively been exhausted (e.g., FS19). Since the photon number spectrum peaks at 𝜈 a for self-absorbed free-free emission, the number of photons with 𝜈 < 𝜈 a is negligible. So, even though additional photons from below 𝜈 a can be brought to the peak for 𝑦 > ∼ 𝑦 a , their presence does not appreciably change the photon number density at 𝑘𝑇 obs or the value of 𝜉 . For the breakout shell, a value of 𝑦 a , bo ≈ ln ( 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo / ℎ𝜈 a , bo ) is found by setting 𝜈 𝑦, bo = 𝜈 a , bo in equation 77. Note that 𝑦 a is only defined for out-of-equilibrium systems with 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo > ℎ𝜈 a , bo , and otherwise we set it to 0.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If 𝑦 > ∼ 𝑦 sat ( 𝜈 𝑦 < 𝜈 coh ), then the 𝑦 -parameter is sufficiently large that the spectrum's shape is significantly affected. The spectrum saturates to a Wien spectrum for 𝑦 ≫ 𝑦 sat . Margalit, Quataert, & Ho (2022), who studied Comptonization of the X-ray emission in interacting supernovae, found that for 𝑦 < ∼ 𝑦 sat , the shape of the free-free seed spectrum is not significantly altered, other than the peak energy shifting to ≈ 4 𝑘𝑇 (see their Figure 8). To estimate 𝑦 sat for the breakout shell, we first note that for ℎ𝜈 coh ≪ 𝑘𝑇 obs , the value of 𝜈 coh is approximated by 𝜈 coh ≈ 𝜈 a / 𝑦 1 / 2 (e.g., Rybicki & Lightman 1986). Plugging 𝜈 𝑦 = 𝜈 coh into equation 77 then yields 𝑦 sat , bo ≈ 𝑦 a , bo + 1 2 ln 𝑦 bo . Note that 𝑦 sat , bo is only defined when 𝑦 bo ≥ 1 and it always satisfies 𝑦 sat , bo > 𝑦 a , bo . For the values of 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo , ℎ𝜈 a , bo , and 𝑦 bo in equations 8, 10, and 76, we find 𝑦 sat , bo ∼ 10. Thus, marginally 𝑦 bo < ∼ 𝑦 sat , bo in our typical case, and as desired the shape of the spectrum should not be greatly affected.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now have three equations (8, 76, and 78) relating the four quantities 𝑣 bo , 𝑇 obs , bo , 𝑦 bo , and 𝜉 bo . We can therefore express any three of them in terms of the fourth. It is instructive to choose either 𝑣 bo or 𝑇 obs , bo as the independent quantity; the former is useful for theory as it has the clearest relation to the properties of the progenitor andexplosion, while the latter is best when comparing to observations since 𝑇 obs , bo is directly observable. Here we will study 𝑇 obs , bo , 𝑦 bo , and 𝜉 bo as functions of 𝑣 bo .
texts.text: We now have three equations (8, 76, and 78) relating the four quantities 𝑣 bo , 𝑇 obs , bo , 𝑦 bo , and 𝜉 bo . We can therefore express any three of them in terms of the fourth. It is instructive to choose either 𝑣 bo or 𝑇 obs , bo as the independent quantity; the former is useful for theory as it has the clearest relation to the properties of the progenitor andexplosion, while the latter is best when comparing to observations since 𝑇 obs , bo is directly observable. Here we will study 𝑇 obs , bo , 𝑦 bo , and 𝜉 bo as functions of 𝑣 bo .

We therefore conclude that our assumption of unsaturated Comptonization is valid, and equation 78 is applicable.

Comptonization effects start to become relevant only above temperatures of a few keV. We therefore expect shock breakout-powered transients peaking in the Swift XRT band or the Einstein Probe bands to be only modestly influenced by Comptonization.

texts.orig: The results of this section justify our assumption of order-unity 𝜉 bo in Sections 4.1-4.3. Even if 𝜉 bo is a few, it is unlikely that our results will be significantly affected. The main effect would be to slightly alter the power-law indices for the time evolution of 𝑇 obs and 𝜈 a , as discussed at the end of Section 2.2, which would in turn slightly modify the spectral indices appearing in Fig. 8.
texts.text: The results of this section justify our assumption of order-unity 𝜉 bo in Sections 4.1-4.3. Even if 𝜉 bo is a few, it is unlikely that our results will be significantly affected. The main effect would be to slightly alter the power-law indices for the time evolution of 𝑇 obs and 𝜈 a , as discussed at the end of Section 2.2, which would in turn slightly modify the spectral indices appearing in Fig. 8.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a further check that our assumptions about the spectrum are reasonable, our results can be compared to the spectra seen in MonteCarlo radiative transfer simulations, for example those of Ito, Levinson, & Nagataki (2020) and Ito, Levinson, & Nakar (2020). In particular, the wind breakout model presented in Ito, Levinson, & Nakar (2020) is relevant. The top panel of their Figure 7 shows spectra for a case with a shock velocity of 0 . 1 c. Although they considered a somewhat higher number density than us, 𝑛 = 10 15 cm -3 , they also pointed out that another model with 𝑛 = 10 12 cm -3 was not significantly different in terms of spectral shape or peak energy, except for a lower self-absorption frequency. Since our simple treatment does not consider the effect that the escape of radiation has on the shock structure, our model should be compared with their models with 𝑓 esc ≪ 1 (for further discussion of the escape fraction, see, e.g., Ioka, Levinson, & Nakar 2019, and references therein). In particular, the 𝑓 esc = 0 . 09 case in Ito, Levinson, & Nakar (2020) exhibits a rather flat spectrum, with only modest Comptonization, and it is self-absorbed below ∼ 1 eV even in spite of their higher assumed density. All of these features are consistent with our model assumptions. The peak energy in their simulation is 3 𝑘𝑇 obs ≈ 3 keV for a shock velocity of 0 . 1 𝑐 , which can be compared with our model to estimate an appropriate value of 𝑞 /( 𝑛 + 1 ) (in their 0 . 25 𝑐 and 0 . 5 𝑐 models, pairs are likely to be important so our model cannot be reliably applied). Using equation 79, for 𝑣 bo = 0 . 1 𝑐 we find 𝑘𝑇 obs , bo = ( 0 . 6 , 1 . 0 , 1 . 7 , 2 . 6 ) keV for 𝑞 /( 𝑛 + 1 ) = ( 0 . 3 , 0 . 5 , 1 , 2 ) . A value of 𝑞 /( 𝑛 + 1 ) = 0 . 5 therefore seems to give a good agreement with the Monte-Carlo simulations of Ito, Levinson, & Nakar (2020), and for typical values of 𝑛 this is consistent with 𝑞 ∼ 1. We note that if photon escape effects are important, the low-frequency spectrum could be somewhat steeper than what we assume. However, at least in the case of GRB 060218, a flat spectrum with a photon index of -1 . 4 to -1 . 1 is observationally indicated (e.g., Toma et al. 2007).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To wrap up this discussion, let us consider what happens after the breakout, as deeper and colder layers are revealed. As discussed above, for a given 𝜈 a and 𝑇 obs , the allowed values of 𝜉 are bounded between 1 ≤ 𝜉 ≤ 𝜉 a . Now, once ejecta near thermal equilibrium become visible, ℎ𝜈 a → 𝑘𝑇 obs , so 𝑦 a → 0 and 𝜉 a → 1. Thus, as expected, we find that 𝜉 → 1 when the system attains thermal equilibrium, and Comptonization eventually becomes unimportant.
texts.text: To wrap up this discussion, let us consider what happens after the breakout, as deeper and colder layers are revealed. As discussed above, for a given 𝜈 a and 𝑇 obs , the allowed values of 𝜉 are bounded between 1 ≤ 𝜉 ≤ 𝜉 a . Now, once ejecta near thermal equilibrium become visible, ℎ𝜈 a → 𝑘𝑇 obs , so 𝑦 a → 0 and 𝜉 a → 1. Thus, as expected, we find that 𝜉 → 1 when the system attains thermal equilibrium, and Comptonization eventually becomes unimportant.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS
texts.text: 5 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Utilizing recent advances in shock breakout theory which predict that the observed temperature falls off more steeply than previously thought at early times (FS19), we have shown that a new type of shock breakout can occur under certain conditions (see Section 2). In this previously unexplored scenario, the emission initially originates from outer regions of the flow where the gas and radiation are out of equilibrium, but deeper regions in thermal equilibrium are able to be probed on a time-scale 𝑡 eq , ls which is shorter than the lightcrossing time of the system 𝑡 lc . The observed spectrum in this regime is significantly different from the standard shock breakout case: it contains both a blackbody component, and a Comptonized free-free component which is smeared out due to the non-negligible lightcrossing time. We call this new shock breakout regime the INERT ('initially non-equilibrium rapid thermalization') regime.
texts.text: Utilizing recent advances in shock breakout theory which predict that the observed temperature falls off more steeply than previously thought at early times (FS19), we have shown that a new type of shock breakout can occur under certain conditions (see Section 2). In this previously unexplored scenario, the emission initially originates from outer regions of the flow where the gas and radiation are out of equilibrium, but deeper regions in thermal equilibrium are able to be probed on a time-scale 𝑡 eq , ls which is shorter than the lightcrossing time of the system 𝑡 lc . The observed spectrum in this regime is significantly different from the standard shock breakout case: it contains both a blackbody component, and a Comptonized free-free component which is smeared out due to the non-negligible lightcrossing time. We call this new shock breakout regime the INERT ('initially non-equilibrium rapid thermalization') regime.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We studied the necessary conditions to obtain this novel type of breakout in Section 3, finding that it depends strongly on the breakout velocity, 𝑣 bo , and preferentially occurs for 𝑣 bo ≈ 0 . 1 𝑐 (to within a factor of ∼ 2 depending on other parameters). A sufficiently high breakout density, such that 𝜌 bo , -11 > ∼ 0 . 4 𝑅 -15 / 16 bo , 14 where 𝑅 bo is the breakout radius, is also required. Motivated by observations of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs which suggest potentially extended progenitors, we also considered the case where the breakout takes place in an extended low-mass medium in Section 3.2, and investigated the dependence on the outer density profile of the medium, i.e. on the parameter 𝑛 in 𝜌 ( 𝑟 ) ∝ ( 𝑅 -𝑟 ) 𝑛 . We constructed a model that is sufficiently general to encapsulate both the classical case of breakout from the edge of a polytropic medium (for order-unity 𝑛 ), and the case of breakout from the edge of a compact stellar wind or CSM (for 𝑛 ≪ 1), as discussed in Sections 3.3 and 3.4. The small 𝑛 limit was determined to be advantageous for obtaining an INERT breakout. We also demonstrated that in this limit, an early optical peak due to cooling emission is expected, as suggested by Nakar & Piro (2014). The inferred properties of the peak are similar to those obtained in other studies of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs (e.g., Nakar 2015; Irwin & Chevalier 2016).
texts.text: We studied the necessary conditions to obtain this novel type of breakout in Section 3, finding that it depends strongly on the breakout velocity, 𝑣 bo , and preferentially occurs for 𝑣 bo ≈ 0 . 1 𝑐 (to within a factor of ∼ 2 depending on other parameters). A sufficiently high breakout density, such that 𝜌 bo , -11 > ∼ 0 . 4 𝑅 -15 / 16 bo , 14 where 𝑅 bo is the breakout radius, is also required. Motivated by observations of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs which suggest potentially extended progenitors, we also considered the case where the breakout takes place in an extended low-mass medium in Section 3.2, and investigated the dependence on the outer density profile of the medium, i.e. on the parameter 𝑛 in 𝜌 ( 𝑟 ) ∝ ( 𝑅 -𝑟 ) 𝑛 . We constructed a model that is sufficiently general to encapsulate both the classical case of breakout from the edge of a polytropic medium (for order-unity 𝑛 ), and the case of breakout from the edge of a compact stellar wind or CSM (for 𝑛 ≪ 1), as discussed in Sections 3.3 and 3.4. The small 𝑛 limit was determined to be advantageous for obtaining an INERT breakout. We also demonstrated that in this limit, an early optical peak due to cooling emission is expected, as suggested by Nakar & Piro (2014). The inferred properties of the peak are similar to those obtained in other studies of 𝑙𝑙 GRBs (e.g., Nakar 2015; Irwin & Chevalier 2016).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 4, we constructed a simple spectral model for a shock breakout in this unexplored regime. The model depends on six parameters - the diffusion time of the breakout shell 𝑡 bo , the light-crossing time 𝑡 lc , the equilibrium time 𝑡 eq , ls , the luminosity 𝐿 bo , the observed temperature 𝑇 obs , bo , and the temporal power-law index 𝛼 describing the temperature evolution - all of which can be directly constrained by observations (for an example, see Paper III). In the INERT regime, for which 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 lc , the emergent spectrum features both a free-free component, smeared into a multi-colour power-law by the effects of light travel time, and a less-smeared blackbody component which is initially subdominant, but eventually comes to dominate (see Fig. 8). The free-free component is initially very broad, with a self-absorption frequency of ℎ𝜈 a ∼ 0 . 1 eV and a temperature of 𝑘𝑇 obs ∼ 10 keV, resulting in strong emission across the electromagnetic spectrum at early times. The blackbody component peaks in the soft X-ray range, typically around ∼ 0 . 1 keV. If the observed temperature decreases as 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -𝛼 , then due to differences in light arrival time, the free-free emission is smeared to form a broken power-law spectrum, with characteristic spectral indices of ( 3 𝛼 -1 )/[ 𝜆 ( 3 𝛼 -2 )] at low frequencies, and ( 1 -3 𝛼 )/( 3 𝛼 ) at high frequencies, as shown in Fig. 8 (for further discussion, refer to Paper II). The high energy index is in agreement with previous studies (e.g., NS10, FS19). We provide estimates for the radius, density, and velocity of the breakout shell in terms of observables, as well as two closure relations which can be used to test whether shock breakout can plausibly explain a given event.

In the INERT regime, for which 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 eq , ls < 𝑡 lc , the emergent spectrum features both a free-free component, smeared into a multi-colour power-law by the effects of light travel time, and a less-smeared blackbody component which is initially subdominant, but eventually comes to dominate (see Fig. 8). The free-free component is initially very broad, with a self-absorption frequency of ℎ𝜈 a ∼ 0 . 1 eV and a temperature of 𝑘𝑇 obs ∼ 10 keV, resulting in strong emission across the electromagnetic spectrum at early times. The blackbody component peaks in the soft X-ray range, typically around ∼ 0 . 1 keV. If the observed temperature decreases as 𝑇 obs ∝ 𝑡 -𝛼 , then due to differences in light arrival time, the free-free emission is smeared to form a broken power-law spectrum, with characteristic spectral indices of ( 3 𝛼 -1 )/[ 𝜆 ( 3 𝛼 -2 )] at low frequencies, and ( 1 -3 𝛼 )/( 3 𝛼 ) at high frequencies, as shown in Fig. 8 (for further discussion, refer to Paper II). The high energy index is in agreement with previous studies (e.g., NS10, FS19). We provide estimates for the radius, density, and velocity of the breakout shell in terms of observables, as well as two closure relations which can be used to test whether shock breakout can plausibly explain a given event.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In terms of applications, the most interesting seems to be 𝑙𝑙 GRBs, as they exemplify the subrelativistic velocities ( ≈ 0 . 1 𝑐 ) and ∼ 10 keV temperatures for which the physics studied here are most relevant. Indeed, the qualitative similarities between the model proposed here and the most well-studied 𝑙𝑙 GRB, GRB 060218, are striking. Many key features of our model, including strong early optical emission, fast early temperature evolution, an optical peak on a time-scale of half a day, and a spectrum with a blackbody hump and a power-law tail, were also exhibited by this peculiar GRB. The rich connection between this work and 𝑙𝑙 GRBs is studied more quantitatively in a subsequent paper (Paper III).
texts.text: In terms of applications, the most interesting seems to be 𝑙𝑙 GRBs, as they exemplify the subrelativistic velocities ( ≈ 0 . 1 𝑐 ) and ∼ 10 keV temperatures for which the physics studied here are most relevant. Indeed, the qualitative similarities between the model proposed here and the most well-studied 𝑙𝑙 GRB, GRB 060218, are striking. Many key features of our model, including strong early optical emission, fast early temperature evolution, an optical peak on a time-scale of half a day, and a spectrum with a blackbody hump and a power-law tail, were also exhibited by this peculiar GRB. The rich connection between this work and 𝑙𝑙 GRBs is studied more quantitatively in a subsequent paper (Paper III).

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: An unexplored regime of shock breakout
texts.text: An unexplored regime of shock breakout

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in Section 3.4, explosions occurring in extended envelopes with the requisite properties for INERT breakout are also expected to result in a peak in the optical light curve on a time-scale of hours to days. We therefore predict that events exhibiting INERT breakout features in X-rays will often be associated with doublepeaked SNe. Interestingly, two 𝑙𝑙 GRBs, GRB 060218 (Campana et al. 2006) and GRB 171205A (Izzo et al. 2019), both of which had distinct thermal and non-thermal components in their spectra, have been conclusively linked to double-peaked SNe. In a third event, GRB 100316D, the existence of an early optical peak could not be established, but excess blue emission at early times hinted that such a peak may have been missed (Cano et al. 2011). Yet another double-peaked event, the broad-lined Type Ic SN 2020bvc, had no detected prompt gamma-ray counterpart, but exhibited a radio and X-ray afterglow markedly similar to GRB 060218 (Ho et al. 2020). The recently discovered soft X-ray transient EP240414a was also associated with a double-peaked Type Ic-bl SN (Sun et al. 2024; van Dalen et al. 2024). Based on these events, the connection between peculiar X-ray transients and double-peaked SNe seems promising, but so far there are not enough well-studied 𝑙𝑙 GRBs to form a proper statistical sample.
texts.text: As discussed in Section 3.4, explosions occurring in extended envelopes with the requisite properties for INERT breakout are also expected to result in a peak in the optical light curve on a time-scale of hours to days. We therefore predict that events exhibiting INERT breakout features in X-rays will often be associated with doublepeaked SNe. Interestingly, two 𝑙𝑙 GRBs, GRB 060218 (Campana et al. 2006) and GRB 171205A (Izzo et al. 2019), both of which had distinct thermal and non-thermal components in their spectra, have been conclusively linked to double-peaked SNe. In a third event, GRB 100316D, the existence of an early optical peak could not be established, but excess blue emission at early times hinted that such a peak may have been missed (Cano et al. 2011). Yet another double-peaked event, the broad-lined Type Ic SN 2020bvc, had no detected prompt gamma-ray counterpart, but exhibited a radio and X-ray afterglow markedly similar to GRB 060218 (Ho et al. 2020). The recently discovered soft X-ray transient EP240414a was also associated with a double-peaked Type Ic-bl SN (Sun et al. 2024; van Dalen et al. 2024). Based on these events, the connection between peculiar X-ray transients and double-peaked SNe seems promising, but so far there are not enough well-studied 𝑙𝑙 GRBs to form a proper statistical sample.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thankfully, Einstein Probe should dramatically increase the number of known 𝑙𝑙 GRBs in the coming years. However, although it will be an excellent tool for discovering X-ray transients of interest, Einstein Probe alone will not suffice to test our spectral model due to its narrow wavelength coverage. A joint detection of a nearby 𝑙𝑙 GRB by Einstein Probe and Swift , SVOM , or Fermi would be ideal, although these satellites could only detect relatively nearby 𝑙𝑙 GRBs. Rapid UV/optical/IR follow-up is also essential, and upcoming highcadence UV missions like ULTRASAT and UVEX will further contribute by providing crucial constraints in the far UV. All of these missions will help us to piece together a complete picture of the early spectrum of stellar transients, providing at long last a chance to rigorously test shock breakout theory. We look forward to these and other missions advancing our understanding of shock breakout physics in the near future.

All of these missions will help us to piece together a complete picture of the early spectrum of stellar transients, providing at long last a chance to rigorously test shock breakout theory. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: APPENDIX B: COMPARISON OF OUR SCALING RELATIONS WITH PAST WORK
texts.text: APPENDIX B: COMPARISON OF OUR SCALING RELATIONS WITH PAST WORK

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We parametrize our envelope breakout model for 𝑛 = 0 in terms of the envelope mass, 𝑀 env, the envelope radius, 𝑅 env, and the energy deposited in the envelope, 𝐸 0 . In Section 3.4, we gave scalings for the observables 𝑡 bo , 𝐿 bo , and 𝐸 bo in terms of these parameters. Here we confirm that our scalings are identical to those of Chevalier & Irwin (2011) and Haynie & Piro (2021).
texts.text: We parametrize our envelope breakout model for 𝑛 = 0 in terms of the envelope mass, 𝑀 env, the envelope radius, 𝑅 env, and the energy deposited in the envelope, 𝐸 0 . In Section 3.4, we gave scalings for the observables 𝑡 bo , 𝐿 bo , and 𝐸 bo in terms of these parameters. Here we confirm that our scalings are identical to those of Chevalier & Irwin (2011) and Haynie & Piro (2021).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Haynie & Piro (2021) instead parametrize the observables in terms of 𝑅 env, the shock velocity 𝑣 s , and the wind parameter 𝐷 defined by 𝜌 ( 𝑟 ) = 𝐷𝑟 -2 . With the replacements 𝑣 s ∝ 𝐸 1 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env and 𝐷 ∝ 𝑀 env 𝑅 -1 env , we obtain 𝑡 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 2 env 𝐷 -1 𝑣 -2 s , 𝐸 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 2 env 𝑣 s , and 𝐿 bo ∝ 𝐷𝑣 3 s , in agreement with the scalings presented in Haynie & Piro (2021). 15
texts.text: Haynie & Piro (2021) instead parametrize the observables in terms of 𝑅 env, the shock velocity 𝑣 s , and the wind parameter 𝐷 defined by 𝜌 ( 𝑟 ) = 𝐷𝑟 -2 . With the replacements 𝑣 s ∝ 𝐸 1 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env and 𝐷 ∝ 𝑀 env 𝑅 -1 env , we obtain 𝑡 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 2 env 𝐷 -1 𝑣 -2 s , 𝐸 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 2 env 𝑣 s , and 𝐿 bo ∝ 𝐷𝑣 3 s , in agreement with the scalings presented in Haynie & Piro (2021). 15

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chevalier & Irwin (2011) also used 𝐷 and 𝑅 env as free parameters, but replaced 𝑣 s with the supernova energy 𝐸 SN and the supernova ejecta mass 𝑀 e via the relation 𝑣 s ∝ ( 𝐸 SN / 𝑀 e ) 1 / 2 ( 𝑀 e / 𝑀 env ) 1 / 4 , which comes from the Chevalier (1983) self-similar solution for an ejecta with an outer density profile 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -7 colliding with a stellar wind with 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -2 . Note that the energy 𝐸 SN, which is the total supernova energy, is different from the energy 𝐸 0 in our model, which represents only the energy deposited in the low-mass envelope; the two are related by 𝐸 0 ≈ 𝐸 SN ( 𝑀 env / 𝑀 e ) 1 / 2 , assuming the same ejecta density profile as above. When this relation and 𝐷 ∝ 𝑀 env / 𝑅 env are substituted into our scaling relations, we obtain 𝑡 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 5 / 2 env 𝐸 -1 SN 𝑀 1 / 2 e 𝐷 -1 / 2
texts.text: Chevalier & Irwin (2011) also used 𝐷 and 𝑅 env as free parameters, but replaced 𝑣 s with the supernova energy 𝐸 SN and the supernova ejecta mass 𝑀 e via the relation 𝑣 s ∝ ( 𝐸 SN / 𝑀 e ) 1 / 2 ( 𝑀 e / 𝑀 env ) 1 / 4 , which comes from the Chevalier (1983) self-similar solution for an ejecta with an outer density profile 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -7 colliding with a stellar wind with 𝜌 ∝ 𝑟 -2 . Note that the energy 𝐸 SN, which is the total supernova energy, is different from the energy 𝐸 0 in our model, which represents only the energy deposited in the low-mass envelope; the two are related by 𝐸 0 ≈ 𝐸 SN ( 𝑀 env / 𝑀 e ) 1 / 2 , assuming the same ejecta density profile as above. When this relation and 𝐷 ∝ 𝑀 env / 𝑅 env are substituted into our scaling relations, we obtain 𝑡 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 5 / 2 env 𝐸 -1 SN 𝑀 1 / 2 e 𝐷 -1 / 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: and 𝐸 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 7 / 4 env 𝐸 1 / 2 SN 𝑀 -1 / 4 e 𝐷 -1 / 4 , which combine to give 𝐿 bo ∝ 𝑅 -3 / 4 env 𝐸 3 / 2 SN 𝑀 -3 / 4 e 𝐷 1 / 4 , in agreement with the results of Chevalier & Irwin (2011) in the 𝑅 w < 𝑅 d case (see their erratum, as there was an error in these scalings in their original paper). 16
texts.text: and 𝐸 bo ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 7 / 4 env 𝐸 1 / 2 SN 𝑀 -1 / 4 e 𝐷 -1 / 4 , which combine to give 𝐿 bo ∝ 𝑅 -3 / 4 env 𝐸 3 / 2 SN 𝑀 -3 / 4 e 𝐷 1 / 4 , in agreement with the results of Chevalier & Irwin (2011) in the 𝑅 w < 𝑅 d case (see their erratum, as there was an error in these scalings in their original paper). 16

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in Section 2, the values of 𝐿 bo and 𝑡 bo reflect the intrinsic luminosity and duration of the breakout, which is only equal to the observed luminosity 𝐿 bo when 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 lc so that light travel effects can be neglected. We may also consider the situation where 𝑡 lc > 𝑡 bo , in which case the observed luminosity is given by 𝐿 obs ≈ 𝐿 bo 𝑡 bo / 𝑡 lc . We then have 𝐿 obs ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 env 𝐸 1 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env , 𝐿 obs ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 env 𝑣 s , or 𝐿 obs ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 3 / 4 env 𝐸 1 / 2 SN 𝑀 -1 / 4 e 𝐷 -1 / 4 , respectively for the parametrizations of this paper, Haynie & Piro (2021), and Chevalier & Irwin (2011).
texts.text: As discussed in Section 2, the values of 𝐿 bo and 𝑡 bo reflect the intrinsic luminosity and duration of the breakout, which is only equal to the observed luminosity 𝐿 bo when 𝑡 bo < 𝑡 lc so that light travel effects can be neglected. We may also consider the situation where 𝑡 lc > 𝑡 bo , in which case the observed luminosity is given by 𝐿 obs ≈ 𝐿 bo 𝑡 bo / 𝑡 lc . We then have 𝐿 obs ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 env 𝐸 1 / 2 0 𝑀 -1 / 2 env , 𝐿 obs ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 env 𝑣 s , or 𝐿 obs ∝ ( 𝑐 / 𝜅 ) 𝑅 3 / 4 env 𝐸 1 / 2 SN 𝑀 -1 / 4 e 𝐷 -1 / 4 , respectively for the parametrizations of this paper, Haynie & Piro (2021), and Chevalier & Irwin (2011).

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 15 In Haynie & Piro (2021), 𝑅 env, 𝑡 bo , 𝐸 bo , and 𝐿 bo are called 𝑅 w, 𝑡 rise , 𝐸 sbo , and 𝐿 sbo respectively.
texts.text: 15 In Haynie & Piro (2021), 𝑅 env, 𝑡 bo , 𝐸 bo , and 𝐿 bo are called 𝑅 w, 𝑡 rise , 𝐸 sbo , and 𝐿 sbo respectively.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 16 In Chevalier & Irwin (2011), 𝐸 SN, 𝑅 env, 𝑡 bo , 𝐸 bo , and 𝐿 bo are called 𝐸 , 𝑅 𝑡 𝐸 𝐿
texts.text: 16 In Chevalier & Irwin (2011), 𝐸 SN, 𝑅 env, 𝑡 bo , 𝐸 bo , and 𝐿 bo are called 𝐸 , 𝑅 𝑡 𝐸 𝐿

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It remains to check that our scalings for 𝑡 p, 𝐿 p, and 𝑇 p given by equations 54-56 reproduce those of Nakar & Piro (2014). Like Chevalier & Irwin (2011), Nakar & Piro (2014) parametrize the problem in terms of the total supernova energy 𝐸 SN, instead of the energy 𝐸 0 which is deposited in the extended enve- lope. 17 In their model, there is a massive core inside of the envelope, and the core mass 𝑀 c ≫ 𝑀 env is also a free parameter. They assume that the transition of the shock from the core to the envelope is described by the self-similar solution of (Sakurai 1960), which results in the relationship 𝐸 0 ∝ 𝐸 SN ( 𝑀 env / 𝑀 c ) 0 . 7 . We then obtain 𝑡 p ∝ 𝜅 0 . 50 𝐸 -0 . 25 SN 𝑀 0 . 17 c 𝑀 0 . 57 env from equa- tion 54, 𝐿 p ∝ 𝜅 -1 𝑅 env 𝐸 SN 𝑀 -0 . 7 c 𝑀 -0 . 3 env from equation 55, and 𝑇 p ∝ 𝜅 -0 . 25 𝑅 0 . 25 env 𝐸 -0 . 13 SN 𝑀 0 . 09 c 𝑀 -0 . 46 env from equation 56. After some rearranging, we find 𝑀 env ∝ 𝐸 0 . 43 SN 𝜅 -0 . 87 𝑀 -0 . 30 c 𝑡 1 . 75 p , 𝑅 env ∝ 𝐸 -0 . 87 SN 𝜅 0 . 74 𝑀 0 . 61 c 𝐿 p 𝑡 0 . 51 p , and 𝑇 p ∝ 𝜅 -1 / 4 𝑅 1 / 4 env 𝑡 -1 / 2 p , which agrees with equations 10, 12, and 13 in Nakar & Piro (2014).
texts.text: It remains to check that our scalings for 𝑡 p, 𝐿 p, and 𝑇 p given by equations 54-56 reproduce those of Nakar & Piro (2014). Like Chevalier & Irwin (2011), Nakar & Piro (2014) parametrize the problem in terms of the total supernova energy 𝐸 SN, instead of the energy 𝐸 0 which is deposited in the extended enve- lope. 17 In their model, there is a massive core inside of the envelope, and the core mass 𝑀 c ≫ 𝑀 env is also a free parameter. They assume that the transition of the shock from the core to the envelope is described by the self-similar solution of (Sakurai 1960), which results in the relationship 𝐸 0 ∝ 𝐸 SN ( 𝑀 env / 𝑀 c ) 0 . 7 . We then obtain 𝑡 p ∝ 𝜅 0 . 50 𝐸 -0 . 25 SN 𝑀 0 . 17 c 𝑀 0 . 57 env from equa- tion 54, 𝐿 p ∝ 𝜅 -1 𝑅 env 𝐸 SN 𝑀 -0 . 7 c 𝑀 -0 . 3 env from equation 55, and 𝑇 p ∝ 𝜅 -0 . 25 𝑅 0 . 25 env 𝐸 -0 . 13 SN 𝑀 0 . 09 c 𝑀 -0 . 46 env from equation 56. After some rearranging, we find 𝑀 env ∝ 𝐸 0 . 43 SN 𝜅 -0 . 87 𝑀 -0 . 30 c 𝑡 1 . 75 p , 𝑅 env ∝ 𝐸 -0 . 87 SN 𝜅 0 . 74 𝑀 0 . 61 c 𝐿 p 𝑡 0 . 51 p , and 𝑇 p ∝ 𝜅 -1 / 4 𝑅 1 / 4 env 𝑡 -1 / 2 p , which agrees with equations 10, 12, and 13 in Nakar & Piro (2014).

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 17 In Nakar & Piro (2014), 𝐸 SN, 𝑣 0 , 𝑅 env, 𝑀 env, and 𝐸 0 are called 𝐸 , 𝑣 ext , 𝑅 ext , 𝑀 ext , and 𝐸 ext , respectively.
texts.text: 17 In Nakar & Piro (2014), 𝐸 SN, 𝑣 0 , 𝑅 env, 𝑀 env, and 𝐸 0 are called 𝐸 , 𝑣 ext , 𝑅 ext , 𝑀 ext , and 𝐸 ext , respectively.
